quote: Changes permitted expression or mixes with it or replaces it
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Must assume three groups differentiated by suppression effectiveness
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Group 1: intention present and perceptible before utterance
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Group 2: suppression prevents perception before speaking
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Group 3: suppression extends over long time, perhaps very long
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Suppressed intention doesn't become perceptible in Group 3
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Cannot be directly denied because not conscious in Group 3
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Suppression of intention to say something is indispensable for slip
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Why doesn't suppression succeed completely or fail completely
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Errors are compromise formations between intentions
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Special unknown conditions must exist for compromise formation
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Must examine other obscure psychic fields for analogies
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Working with small signs brings dangers (paranoia uses excessively)
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Dynamic conception of psychic phenomena (not mere description)
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Perceived phenomena give way to assumed strivings/tendencies
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Dream discovery that disease symptoms have meaning led to psychoanalytic method
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Dream study is best preparation for study of neuroses
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Dream itself is neurotic symptom occurring in all normals

source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Errors were neglected by science but dream carries odium of unscientific
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Dream regarded as ignominious, awakens suspicion of mysticism

quote: Objection that dream too trifling compared to tumors/hemorrhages/inflammations

quote: Dream defies exact research requirements (object not even sure)

quote: Dream cannot be related accurately, changes in telling

quote: Most dreams cannot be remembered, forgotten except fragments
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Question of insignificance already dealt with in discussing errors

quote: Other psychiatric objects suffer same indefiniteness (compulsion ideas)

quote: Historical personages drew inspiration for important deeds from dreams
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Contempt for dream is reaction to overestimation in former times

source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Among Etruscans/Romans other future-discovery methods used but dream interpretation practiced

quote: Art of dream interpretation gradually lost, interest deteriorated into superstition

quote: Exact science dealt with dreams only to apply physiological theories

quote: Scientific description emphasizes deviations from waking thought disparaging dream
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Disintegration of association, suspension of critical faculty, elimination of knowledge
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Only valuable scientific contribution: influence of bodily stimuli during sleep
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: J. Mourly Vold two volumes experimental researches on dreams (Norwegian, 1910/1912)
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Vold's work deals almost solely with consequences of limb position changes
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Exact science would reject finding meaning in dreams
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Psychoanalysis follows ancient dream interpreters despite scientific opposition
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Assume as hypothesis: dream is not somatic but psychic phenomenon
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Dream as psychic phenomenon is achievement/expression of dreamer
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Dream tells us nothing (unintelligible) that we don't understand
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Ask dreamer to give meaning of his dream (same as error investigation)
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Someone said "vorschwein" - asked what meant, immediately answered
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Dreamer himself to discover solutions of riddles (psychoanalytic method)
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Dreamer does know what dream means but doesn't know that he knows
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Believes he doesn't know (same situation as Bernheim hypnosis experiment)
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Two assumptions: dream is psychic; dreamer has unconscious knowledge of it
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Bernheim (Nancy, 1889) placed subjects in somnambulistic state with hallucinations
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Upon waking subject knew nothing of hypnotic experiences
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Subject wavered, began to remember, recollection became complete
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Subject finally knew but had no experiences from other source meanwhile
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Therefore subject knew memories in beginning though inaccessible
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Subject didn't know that he knew, believed he didn't know
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: This proves we may assume dreamer has inaccessible knowledge of dream
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: For errors we asked: how did person happen to use wrong word
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Next idea gave explanation (his next statement taken as explanation)
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Dream technique: ask how dreamer happened to have dream
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Next statement to be taken as explanation (disregard dreamer's belief/disbelief)

quote: Distinction whether dreamer believes he knows irrelevant (treat both same)
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Objection: dreamer's next idea could be anything at all (appears arbitrary)
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: We assume dreamer's association will be determined, not arbitrary
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Belief in psychic freedom is unscientific, must capitulate to determinism

quote: Only one association will occur to person questioned (can be proved)

source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Some elements produce no associations from dreamer (happens but not always)

quote: When no association: contradict dreamer, insist he must have some association
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Any association acceptable: historical information, reminiscences, anything
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Free association controlled by given premise (dream element as starting point)
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Special delimitation of attention required (different from cogitation)
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Higher level: free association without original premise (name or number freely given)
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Such "free" associations appear voluntary but are strongly determined
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Determined by important inner mental set unknown at moment
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Like disturbing tendencies in errors, provocative tendencies in accidents
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Investigations by Freud and others: free name associations analyzed
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Name associations followed by continuous associations revealing motivation
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: Investigations always yield same results, information often covers wealth of material
source: GROUP PSYCHOLOGY AND THE ANALYSIS OF THE EGO

quote: He himself was the Albino, most interesting feminine person to him at moment

In analytic treatment transference becomes subject of treatment
At end of analytic cure transference itself must be abolished
Therefore effect whether positive/negative founded not on suggestion
On inner change achieved in patient which suggestion made possible
Creation of separate suggestions counteracted during cure
Continually forced to attack resistances changing into negative transferences
Large number of results confirmed from other unquestionable sources
As authoritative witnesses refer to testimony of dements and paranoiacs
What they tell about symbolic translations and phantasies agrees with our results
Agrees with results from unconscious of transference-neurotics
Gives added weight to objective correctness of interpretations
Believe will not go wrong giving confidence to analysis with reference to these factors
First because libido not directed toward any real object
Second because must use energy to keep libido suppressed
Would become well if end to conflict between ego and libido
Task of therapy: free libido from present bonds estranging it from ego
Symptoms furnish only available substitute satisfaction at that time
We have to become master of symptoms and abolish them
To abolish symptoms necessary to go back to their origin
But this time with help of motive forces originally not available
Revision of suppression process accomplished only partly by memory traces
Decisive part accomplished by means of relationship to physician (transference)
By transference create new editions of old conflict
Under this situation patient would like to behave as had originally
But by summoning available psychic power compel to reach different decision
Transference becomes battlefield where all contending forces meet
Full strength of libido concentrated in relation to physician
Entire resistance against it also concentrated there
In place of original disturbance patient manifests transference disturbance
In place of heterogeneous unreal objects now only person of physician
New struggle over this object raised to highest psychic level
With physician's suggestions proceeds as normal psychic conflict
By avoiding new suppression, estrangement between ego and libido ends
When libido detaches from temporary physician object
Forces overcome in therapy on one hand: ego's aversion for libido directions
First: all libido forced from symptoms into transference
So libido cannot escape ego by fleeing into unconscious
Accomplished by change in ego under physician's suggestion influence
In course of work translating unconscious into conscious
Becomes inclined to permit some satisfaction for it
By prospect of occupying some libido through sublimation
More processes correspond to theoretic description, greater success
Will not permit object-transference to effect more than just so much
Able to gather entire libido withdrawn from ego control
By drawing part to ourselves in transference process
Cannot reach direct conclusion about libido disposition during disease
From distributions effected during/because of treatment
Assuming succeeded in curing by strong father-transference creation/destruction
Wrong to conclude patient previously suffered similar unconscious attachment to father
Father-transference merely battlefield where overcame libido
Patient's libido concentrated here from other positions
Battlefield need not have coincided with enemy's important fortresses
Defense of hostile capital need not take place before its very gates
Not until destroyed transference can reconstruct libido distribution during illness
From libido theory standpoint last word regarding dream
In form of wish fulfillment show what wish impulses suppressed
Show to what objects libido withdrawn from ego attached
Dream interpretation plays large role in psychoanalytic treatment
In many cases for long time most important means with which we work
Already know sleep condition carries abatement of suppressions
Because of lessening pressure, suppressed impulse can create clearer expression
Dream study easiest approach to knowledge of libidinous suppressed unconscious
Dreams of neurotics differ in no essential point from dreams of normal persons
Would be unreasonable to explain nervous dreams differently than normal
Difference between neurosis and health applies only during day
Necessary to attribute to healthy numerous assumptions from neurotic study
Cannot be in position to deny healthy man possesses factors enabling dream development
Healthy also possess factors enabling symptom development
Must conclude healthy have also made use of suppressions
Put to certain trouble to keep impulses under control
System of their unconscious also conceals suppressed yet energetic impulses
Part of their libido also withdrawn from ego control
But dreams apparently only symptoms he can manifest
Yet if subject waking hours to penetrating analysis
Discover life shot through with number of trivial symptom formations
Difference between nervous health and neurosis entirely practical
Determined by available capacity for enjoyment and accomplishment
Varies presumably with relative proportion of energy totals
Not have to remind this conception theoretical basis for certainty
That neuroses can be cured despite constitutional disposition foundation
Identity between healthy/neurotic dreams for definition of health
Regards dream itself: cannot separate from relation to neurotic symptoms
Must recognize not completely defined as translation into archaic expression
And of object-occupations which actually taken place
Perhaps disappointed I dealt only with theory on psychoanalytic therapy
Said nothing of conditions under which cure undertaken
Shall omit first because intended no practical training
At beginning emphasized under favorable circumstances attain comparable results
Results to happiest achievements in internal therapy field
May add these could not have been otherwise achieved
If said more might be suspected of drowning voices of disparagement
Psychoanalysts repeatedly threatened colleagues would open public's eyes
By statistical collection of analytic failures and injuries
But such collection aside from biased denunciatory character would be worthless
Statistics worthless if collected cases not comparable
Various neuroses we undertook hardly comparable on same basis
Period of time over which could report too short for permanency judgment
Concerning certain cases could not give information whatever
Related to persons who kept ailments and treatment secret
Strongest hindrance in knowledge men behave irrationally in therapy matters
Have no prospect of attaining anything by appeal to reason
Therapeutic novelty received with frenzied enthusiasm
Very obvious prejudice against psychoanalysis existed
When cured difficult case: "that's no proof, would have gotten well anyway"
Patient going through cycles of depression/mania came during cessation
Three weeks later found in beginnings of new attack
All family/medical authorities convinced new attack result of attempted analysis
See it again in prejudices warring nations developed against each other
Most sensible thing: wait and allow time to wear it away
Why they formerly thought otherwise remains dark secret
Possible prejudice against psychoanalysis already on wane
Continual spread of psychoanalytic doctrine vouches for it
Increase of number of physicians treating analytically vouches for it
When young physician caught in storm of outraged feeling toward hypnosis
Today contrasted with psychoanalysis by "sober" men
We psychoanalysts call ourselves its rightful heirs
Have not forgotten large encouragement and theoretical explanation we owe to it
Injuries blamed on psychoanalysis limited to temporary aggravation of conflict
When analysis clumsily handled or broken off unfinished
Can form own opinion whether endeavors likely to lead to lasting injury
Above all transference dangerous remedy in unconscientious physician hands
Keenly depressed over many faults in lectures just delivered
Sorry I often promised to return to subject only slightly touched
Then found context did not make possible to keep word
Undertaken to inform about unfinished thing still in process of development
Often presented evidence then did not draw conclusion
Hysterical symptoms arise from psychical trauma that has been inadequately abreacted
The symptomatology of hysteria can be compared to a pictographic script requiring decipherment
Being sick means disgust in the hysterical pictographic script
Hysterical symptoms have symbolic meaning and represent repressed traumatic experiences
Sexual experiences in childhood are the primary etiological factor in neuroses
Traumatic experiences create a hypnoid state whose products are cut off from associative connection with ego-consciousness
The affect itself can create a hypnoid state that prevents integration of experience
The pressure technique (pressing on the forehead) can elicit repressed memories when hypnosis is not possible
Patients' resistance to revealing certain ideas indicates proximity to pathogenic material
The analyst must pursue chains of association until the pathogenic idea is uncovered
Strong psychical motives can sustain symptoms even when they draw nourishment from organic conditions
Anxiety attacks often result from the repudiation of sensual impulses
Patients' conscious attribution of symptoms to organic causes often conceals psychical etiology
The psychical mechanism of obsessions has great internal kinship with hysterical symptoms
The technique of analysis is the same for both obsessions and hysteria
Isolated words emerging under pressure can reveal pathogenic memories when assembled
The unconscious can produce seemingly random word associations that form coherent pathogenic narratives
There exists an unconscious intelligence that guides the production of symptoms and associations
Sexual content in childhood reading can produce anxiety reactions before full understanding develops
Children can experience frightening reactions to sexual scenes without conscious understanding of their meaning
Witnessing parental sexual activity can produce anxiety attacks in children
Sexual advances by adults toward children create traumatic effects that manifest in later neurotic symptoms
Giving patients information about the traumatic origin of their symptoms can produce immediate therapeutic success
The removal of symptoms requires uncovering and working through the pathogenic ideas that sustain them
Patients often put up remarkably small conscious resistance despite the presence of strong unconscious resistances
Nervous cough can be sustained by psychical motives related to loss and lovelessness
Physical symptoms can symbolically express emotional states and relational deprivation
Stuporose states with rigid limbs can result from sexual trauma in childhood
Sexual abuse by governesses can produce morning attack states in children
Excessive affectionateness between governess and child may indicate inappropriate sexual behavior
Disgust reactions to sexual scenes can produce vomiting that persists for days
The feeling of disgust in response to sexual observations precedes conscious understanding
Earlier sexual experiences gain traumatic force retroactively when later experiences provide understanding
Traumatic moments can illuminate and activate earlier experiences to produce symptoms
Uncle-niece sexual advances constitute a common form of childhood sexual trauma
Witnessing infidelity of family members can trigger neurotic symptoms
Loss of breath can symbolically represent the breathlessness experienced during traumatic sexual witnessing
Hysterical breathlessness preserves the somatic state accompanying the original trauma
Patients may resist psychoanalytic treatment with religious defenses
The analyst must reject patients' own etiological theories when they conflict with psychogenic causation
Traumatic experiences that are not adequately abreacted remain pathogenic
The therapeutic task is to enable abreaction of affects that were originally suppressed
Defense consists in turning a strong idea into a weak one by robbing it of its affect
Pathogenic recollections are recognizable by patients describing them as unimportant while uttering them under resistance
Patients try to disown emerging pathogenic memories by claiming the analyst put them in their head
The analyst must remain unshakeable and recognize memories despite patients' disavowals and resistance
Hysterical patients of a visual type do not make such difficulties for the analyst as those with obsessions
Memory pictures become fragmentary and obscure as the patient describes them because describing converts them into words
When a memory picture remains obstinately before the patient's inward eye despite description, this indicates important material remains untold
Pathogenic psychical material is stratified in concentric layers around a pathogenic nucleus
Symptoms are overdetermined - a single symptom is determined in several ways by multiple pathogenic threads
Pathogenic material is organized according to thought-content through logical threads that take irregular, twisting paths to the nucleus
Pathogenic organization behaves not like a foreign body but like an infiltrate whose external strata pass imperceptibly into the normal ego
Treatment does not consist in extirpating pathogenic material but in causing resistance to melt and enabling circulation into cut-off regions
Only a single memory at a time can enter ego-consciousness through the defile of consciousness
It is hopeless to try to penetrate directly to the nucleus of the pathogenic organization even if the analyst could guess it
The analyst must work from the periphery inward radially while the patient handles the peripheral extension of the work
Gaps and imperfections in the patient's account reveal approaches to material in deeper layers
Neurosis does not relax the laws of logical connection and sufficient motivation in trains of thought
The analyst must reject the theoretical prejudice that hysterics have abnormal brains that throw overboard common psychological laws
Once concealed unconscious motives are discovered, nothing puzzling or contrary to rule remains in hysterical thought connections
Transference onto the physician occurs through a false connection when pathogenic wishes link to the analyst through compulsion to associate
Patients are deceived afresh every time transference repeats, not recognizing the false connection
Transference must be treated as a new symptom produced on the old model and made conscious to the patient
Transference resistances arise from fear of becoming sexually dependent on the physician
Patients must overcome the distressing affect of entertaining forbidden wishes whether in historical or present therapeutic instances
Transferences are compulsions and illusions that melt away with the conclusion of analysis
Failing to make the obstacle of transference clear would give patients a new hysterical symptom in exchange for the spontaneous one
Cathartic psychotherapy gains its justification not from removing pathology but from establishing conditions for healthier functioning
The goal of therapy is transforming hysterical misery into common unhappiness
The splitting of consciousness in defence hysteria results from an act of will on the part of the patient, not from innate weakness
Defence hysteria arises when the ego is faced with an incompatible idea that arouses such distressing affect that the subject decides to forget about it
In females incompatible ideas arise chiefly on the soil of sexual experience and sensation
In defence hysteria, the ego weakens the incompatible idea by transforming its sum of excitation into somatic innervation - this is conversion
The idea that has been weakened through conversion persists as a weak idea in consciousness or recurs during attacks
Hysterical conversion can be either total or partial
Ideational material in hysteria is transformed into chronic symptoms and acute attacks of hysteria
The conversion mechanism of defence hysteria can be arrived at by an effort of will
Defence hysteria and hypnoid hysteria are distinguishable forms with different mechanisms
In obsessions and phobias, separation of the incompatible idea from its affect occurs, but the affect remains in the psychical sphere
The liberated affect attaches itself to other suitable but not incompatible ideas, producing obsessional ideas or phobias
Transposition of affect is the mechanism of defence in obsessions and phobias
The ego gains less advantage from transposition of affect than from hysterical conversion because the affect remains unaltered and undiminished
Patients are astonished by their obsessional affects while the physician finds the affect justified but wrongly attached
Obsessional affects represent dislodged or transposed affects that can be retranslated into sexual terms
Liberated anxiety seizes upon common primary phobias of mankind or upon things unmistakably associated with the sexual
The separation of idea from affect and the false connection are processes that occur without consciousness
The processes of separation and false connection may be physical processes whose psychical consequences present themselves as if psychological changes had occurred
Patients aware of the sexual origin of their obsessions often keep them secret
A third method of defence leads to hallucinatory confusion where the ego rejects the incompatible idea together with its affect and behaves as if the idea had never approached it
In hallucinatory psychosis, the ego breaks away from the incompatible idea but this is inseparably attached to reality, so the ego also detaches itself from reality
The ego creates for itself a wishful psychosis in hallucinatory confusion as a substitute for the intolerable reality
Confusional psychosis is a defence mechanism available only to egos of particularly abnormal constitution
Defence neuropsychoses share the precondition that an incompatible idea has obtruded itself on the ego provoking a defensive reaction
The specific form of neurosis depends on whether the sum of excitation is converted into somatic, transposed onto other ideas, or leads to detachment from reality
The choice between different pathological outcomes depends on constitutional idiosyncrasy or psychical peculiarity
The defensive struggle culminates in a chronic abnormality only if the organic basis is suitable
Defence against an incompatible idea can fail to appear if the nervous system is intact and no serious hereditary taint exists
Whether a neurotic illness occurs depends upon a quantitative factor - the total load on the nervous system compared with its capacity for resistance
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Heredity acts like a multiplier that increases the dimensions of neurosis but does not determine its form
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: What form the neurosis assumes is solely determined by the specific aetiological factor arising from sexual life
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Nervous disorders can develop in people who are perfectly healthy and whose family is above reproach, as seen in neurasthenia
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Hereditary predisposition is merely a precondition of neurosis, not its specific cause - it cannot explain episodic onset or cessation through treatment
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: The specific aetiology of hysteria is a passive sexual experience before puberty
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Hysterical symptoms are always traced back to an event in the subject's sexual life appropriate for producing distressing emotion
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: The specific cause of hysteria is a precocious experience of sexual relations with actual genital excitement from sexual abuse by another person before age eight to ten
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Sexual relations between children often continue for years, with the boy repeating practices to which he was subjected by servants or governesses
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Passive sexual experience occurring after age eight to ten is no longer able to serve as the foundation of neurosis
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Patients never repeat traumatic sexual stories spontaneously - memories must be extracted piece by piece under energetic pressure and against enormous resistance
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: While traumatic sexual memories are being awakened, patients become prey to emotion that would be hard to counterfeit
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: The precocious sexual event leaves an indelible imprint represented by a host of symptoms and special features that could be accounted for in no other way
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Precocious sexual excitation produces little or no effect at the time but its psychical trace is preserved and awakened at puberty
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Thanks to changes at puberty, the memory of childhood sexual trauma displays a power completely lacking from the event itself - operating as though it were a contemporary event
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: The awakening of a sexual memory after puberty when the event happened long before forms the only psychological instance of a memory's effect surpassing that of the actual event
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: This posthumous action of sexual trauma is an abnormal constellation that touches a weak side of the psychical mechanism and produces pathological effect
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: The inverse relation between the psychical effect of memory and of the event contains the reason for the memory remaining unconscious
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Obsessional neurosis differs from hysteria in that sexual memories relate to actions performed with pleasure rather than passive experiences
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: In obsessional neurosis, active sexual experiences in early childhood preceded by passive sexual experiences form the specific aetiology
source: HEREDITY AND THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: The most immediate and practically significant causes of every case of neurotic illness are found in factors arising from sexual life
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: A physician investigating neuroses cannot avoid asking patients about their sexual life and insisting upon receiving a true account
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Objections against inquiring into sexual life express prudery unworthy of a physician that inadequately conceals its weakness behind bad arguments
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: No one ever becomes neurotic through work or excitement alone - intellectual work is rather a protection against neurasthenia
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: The most unremitting intellectual workers remain exempt from neurasthenia
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Sexual aetiology makes it possible to understand all details of neurasthenic clinical history including mysterious improvements and incomprehensible deteriorations
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Removal from home in hydropathic treatment entails interruption of marital intercourse, making temporary elimination of pathogenic cause possible
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: With neurasthenia, therapeutic problems must be attacked within the framework of the patient's life, not in hydropathic institutions
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Masturbation is far commoner among grown-up girls and mature men than generally supposed and produces neurasthenic symptoms
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Coitus interruptus practiced regularly produces neurotic symptoms
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Natural intercourse during pregnancy provides a curative effect on neurosis
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Charcot was not a reflective thinker but an artist-scientist who was a 'visuel' - a man who sees
Charcot's method was to look repeatedly at things he did not understand until suddenly understanding dawned - the apparent chaos gave way to order
Nosography - the intellectual work of seeing new nosological pictures emerge from apparent chaos - was Charcot's particular gift
The greatest satisfaction in science is to see something new - to recognize it as new
Verbal malformations in dreams resemble those in paranoia, hysteria, and obsessions, sharing a common source with linguistic tricks performed by children
Analysis of nonsensical verbal forms in dreams particularly exhibits the dream-work's achievements in condensation
Essential dream-thoughts completely replace the dream and would be sufficient in themselves to replace it if there were no censorship
Dream-thoughts emerge as a complex structure with all the attributes of trains of thought familiar in waking life, often starting from more than one centre
Each train of dream-thought is almost invariably accompanied by its contradictory counterpart, linked by antithetical association
Dreams have no means at their disposal for representing logical relations like 'if', 'because', 'either-or' between dream-thoughts
Dreams disregard logical conjunctions and take over only the substantive content of dream-thoughts - restoration of connections is a task for interpretation
The incapacity of dreams to express logical relations lies in the nature of the psychical material out of which dreams are made, similar to plastic arts compared to poetry
Spoken sentences in dreams are unmodified or slightly modified reproductions of speeches found among recollections in dream-thoughts
Dreams reproduce logical connection by simultaneity in time, combining material into a single situation or event
When dreams show two elements close together, this guarantees some specially intimate connection between what corresponds to them among dream-thoughts
Causal relations are represented in dreams by temporal sequence - either as a sequence of introductory and main dream, or by direct transformation of one image into another
The alternative 'either-or' cannot be expressed in dreams - both alternatives are usually inserted as though they were equally valid
In the great majority of cases, the causal relation is not represented at all but is lost in the confusion of elements in the dreaming process
The most complicated achievements of thought are possible without the assistance of consciousness, as proven by psychoanalysis of hysteria and obsessions
Dream-thoughts may originate from the previous day, proceeding unobserved by consciousness and already completed at sleep onset
Becoming conscious is connected with the application of attention, a function only available in a specific quantity
Symbolism plays a part in dreams from the very first - dreamers have symbolism at their disposal from the earliest age
Temporal repetition of an act is regularly shown in dreams by numerical multiplication of an object
Psychoanalytic research finds no fundamental but only quantitative distinctions between normal and neurotic life
Analysis of dreams shows complete identity in mechanisms and symbolism between healthy and sick individuals - repressed complexes operate alike in both
Naive dreams of healthy people often contain simpler, more perspicuous and characteristic symbolism than those of neurotics
In neurotic dreams, symbolism may be obscure and hard to interpret due to more powerful workings of censorship and more far-reaching dream-distortion
Sexual flower symbolism occurs very commonly and symbolizes human sexual organs by blossoms, which are the sexual organs of plants
Gifts of flowers between lovers may have unconscious sexual meaning representing exchange of sexual gifts
Dreams can express thoughts of which the dreamer is scarcely aware in waking life, particularly thoughts concerned with sensual love and its organs
The meaning of symbols and significance of dreams can be arrived at with particular ease from dreams of people uninitiated into psychoanalysis
Dreams can represent serious thoughts far removed from anything sexual by means of infantile sexual material
In well-formed dreams there is nothing superfluous - every word is a symbol
The dream-work proper is not simply more careless or irrational than waking thought - it is completely different qualitatively and not immediately comparable with it
The dream-work does not think, calculate or judge at all - it restricts itself to giving things a new form
The dream has above all to evade censorship, and the dream-work uses displacement of psychical intensities to the point of a transvaluation of all psychical values
Thoughts must be reproduced exclusively or predominantly in visual and acoustic memory-traces, imposing considerations of representability on the dream-work
Extensive condensation serves to produce greater intensities than are available in dream-thoughts at night
Little attention is paid to logical relations between thoughts - those relations are given a disguised representation in certain formal characteristics of dreams
Affects attached to dream-thoughts undergo less modification than their ideational content - affects are suppressed or detached from proper ideas
Dreams are nothing other than a particular form of thinking made possible by conditions of the state of sleep - it is the dream-work which creates that form and is the essence of dreaming
Every dream contains the fulfillment of a wish - the dream was preferred to waking reflection because it could show the wished-for situation
For the sake of wish fulfillment, sleep can be prolonged by one moment - the dream shows what is wished for as still present
Many analysts wrongly seek the essence of dreams in latent content, overlooking the distinction between latent dream-thoughts and dream-work
The content of dreams must have been overdetermined - words spoken in dreams are made up of words actually spoken in lifetime connected with important events
Memory of dreams is not only fragmentary but positively inaccurate and falsified - we may fill in what was never there with new material
Despite untrustworthiness of memory, it is just as important to interpret the smallest, least conspicuous and most uncertain constituents of dreams as the most clearly preserved
Precisely the most trivial elements of a dream are indispensable to its interpretation - work is held up if attention is not paid to them
We treat dream texts as Holy Writ what previous writers regarded as arbitrary improvisation hurriedly patched together
No conclusions upon construction and working methods of the mental instrument can be fully proved from investigation of dreams or any other mental function taken in isolation
Psychological hypotheses from dream analysis must remain in suspense until related to findings from other enquiries approaching the same problem from another angle
Dreams make use of the present tense in the same manner as day-dreams - the present tense is that in which wishes are represented as fulfilled
Not every dream exhibits transformation from idea into sensory image - there are dreams consisting only of thoughts which cannot be denied the essential nature of dreams
Transformation of ideas into sensory images also occurs in hallucinations and visions in health and as symptoms in psychoneuroses - this relation is not exclusive to dreams
The scene of action of dreams is different from that of waking ideational life - this makes the special peculiarities of dream-life intelligible
We picture the mental apparatus as a compound instrument of 'systems' arranged in temporal sequence through which excitation passes
All psychical activity starts from stimuli and ends in innervations - the apparatus has a sensory and motor end with a sense or direction
Reflex processes remain the model of every psychical function - the psychical apparatus must be constructed like a reflex apparatus
We must distribute perception and memory functions onto different systems - a system receives perceptions but retains no trace, while mnemic systems retain permanent modifications
Association consists in the fact that excitation is transmitted more readily from one mnemic element to another due to diminished resistances and facilitating paths
The perception system without capacity to retain modifications provides consciousness with sensory qualities, while memories even deeply stamped are themselves unconscious
What we describe as our character is based on memory-traces of impressions, especially those of earliest youth which scarcely ever become conscious
Memories can produce all their effects while in an unconscious condition - when they become conscious they exhibit no sensory quality or very slight quality
Formation of dreams required the hypothesis of two psychical agencies - one submitting the other's activity to criticism involving exclusion from consciousness
The critical agency stands closer to consciousness like a screen between the criticized agency and consciousness, and directs waking life and voluntary actions
The preconscious system allows excitatory processes to enter consciousness without impediment if certain conditions are fulfilled - it holds the key to voluntary movement
The unconscious system has no access to consciousness except via the preconscious, through which its excitatory process must submit to modifications
The motive force for producing dreams is supplied by the unconscious system - the dream-wish originates from the Ucs
The unconscious must be assumed to be the general basis of psychical life, as the larger sphere which includes within it the smaller sphere of the conscious
Everything conscious has an unconscious preliminary stage; whereas what is unconscious may remain at that stage and nevertheless claim to be regarded as having the full value of a psychical process
The unconscious is the true psychical reality; in its innermost nature it is as much unknown to us as the reality of the external world
Dreams prove that what is suppressed continues to exist in normal people as well as abnormal, and remains capable of psychical functioning
The interpretation of dreams is the royal road to a knowledge of the unconscious activities of the mind

Functional illnesses do not presuppose the disintegration of the apparatus or the production of fresh splits, but are to be explained dynamically by the strengthening and weakening of components in the interplay of forces
Consciousness functions as a sense-organ for the perception of psychical qualities
The Cs. system resembles the perceptual systems in being susceptible to excitation by qualities but incapable of retaining traces of alterations - that is, as having no memory
Consciousness perceives new qualities and makes a new contribution to directing the mobile quantities of cathexis and distributing them in an expedient fashion

The unpleasure principle regulates the displacement of cathexes automatically, but consciousness may introduce a second and more discriminating regulation which perfects the efficiency of the apparatus
Thought-processes acquire quality by being associated with verbal memories, whose residues of quality draw the attention of consciousness to them
Repression affects memories more easily than perceptions because memories can receive no extra cathexis from the excitation of the psychical sense-organs
The respect paid to dreams in antiquity is based upon correct psychological insight and is homage to the uncontrolled and indestructible forces in the human mind

What we have discovered by the analysis of dreams is the existence of two fundamentally different kinds of psychical process (primary and secondary), and the fact that these two kinds cooperate in the production of our mental acts
Dream-work shows the same processes (condensation, displacement, etc.) that operate in the formation of hysterical symptoms, proving these mechanisms are present in the normal mental apparatus
Dreams function as guardians of sleep by providing psychical consummation for suppressed wishes through representing them as fulfilled
The dream provides a compromise which simultaneously satisfies both the suppressed wish and the agency maintaining sleep
Most dreams of adults are traced back by analysis to erotic wishes, because no other group of instincts has been submitted to such far-reaching suppression by cultural education

Almost every civilized man retains the infantile forms of sexual life in some respect or other, making repressed infantile sexual wishes the most frequent and strongest motive-forces for dream construction
Dream-symbols are used by dreamers speaking the same language, and some extend beyond language use, though dreamers themselves are unaware of the symbols' meaning
The forgetting of proper names with substitute names (paramnesia) follows discoverable paths conforming to laws rather than arbitrary psychical choice
The displacement leading to substitute names is not left to arbitrary choice but follows predictable paths, with substitute names connected in discoverable ways to the missing name

Forgetting of names occurs when a motive causes suppression of connected thoughts, and this suppression associates with the name through external connection, causing the name to be forgotten against one's will

Substitute names represent a compromise, reminding one both of what one wanted to forget and what one wanted to remember, showing that the intention to forget was neither complete success nor complete failure

Conditions for forgetting a name with paramnesia include: (1) a disposition for forgetting the name, (2) a process of suppression shortly before, (3) possibility of external association between the name and the previously suppressed element
A suppressed element always strives to assert itself elsewhere, but is successful only when suitable conditions meet it halfway

Phenomena of daily life such as forgetting, slips of the tongue, and bungled actions provide a basis for psychopathology in psychology, showing the same mechanisms operate in normal and pathological mental life
Parapraxes (Freudian slips) are not chance events but result from the interference of two different intentions, one of which is kept from consciousness
Slips of the tongue reveal thoughts the speaker wished to suppress, demonstrating that suppressed material finds indirect pathways to expression
There is no such thing as arbitrary determination in the mind - even apparently chance psychical events follow deterministic laws
Forgetting is often motivated by unpleasure - we most easily forget what is associated with feelings of unpleasure or what would awaken such feelings if recalled
Bungled actions (symptomatic acts) often represent compromises between conscious intentions and unconscious counter-intentions
The same psychical mechanisms revealed in neurotic symptoms operate in the everyday mistakes, forgetting, and slips of normal people
Apparently meaningless and arbitrary psychical products always have motivation and significance when subjected to psychoanalytic investigation
The forgetting of names, words, and impressions can regularly be traced to the influence of emotional complexes that remain unconscious
Superstition is largely concerned with anticipation of misfortune, representing projection of unconscious hostile and envious wishes outward onto external fate
The psychopathology of everyday life demonstrates the continuity between normal and abnormal mental functioning, with the same fundamental mechanisms operative in both

Popular opinion's view of the sexual instinct - that it is absent in childhood, sets in at puberty, and aims at sexual union - gives a very false picture containing numerous errors and hasty conclusions
Sexual object (the person from whom attraction proceeds) and sexual aim (the act toward which the instinct tends) both show numerous deviations from what is assumed to be normal
Inversion (homosexuality) demonstrates that sexual object choice is not fixed by nature to the opposite sex, as popular fables suggest
The sexual instinct and the sexual object are merely soldered together, and this connection is looser than popular assumption suggests
The findings of psychoanalysis are based entirely upon everyday medical observation, to which psychoanalytic research lends additional depth and scientific significance
The work deliberately remains independent of biological findings, aiming to discover how far psychological investigation can throw light upon the biology of human sexual life
Psychoanalytic method may legitimately lead to opinions differing largely from those based on biological considerations
Preference is given to accidental factors over disposition, and to ontogenesis over phylogenesis, because accidental factors play the principal part in analysis and are subject to its influence
The enlarged concept of sexuality in psychoanalysis coincides with the Eros of Plato, demonstrating classical precedent for broad conceptions of sexual instinct

Inverts vary in behavior from absolute inverts (exclusively same-sex objects) to amphigenic inverts (psychosexual hermaphrodites with objects of either sex) to contingent inverts (capable of same-sex relations under certain conditions)

Inversion cannot be regarded as degeneracy in the legitimate sense, as it is found in people with no other serious deviations, in those of high intellectual development and ethical culture, and was prevalent in ancient civilizations

The indiscriminate use of the concept of degeneracy adds nothing to knowledge; degeneracy should only be attributed where several serious deviations are found together and capacity for functioning is severely impaired
The choice between 'innate' and 'acquired' does not cover all issues involved in inversion - both factors must cooperate rather than being mutually exclusive explanations
External influences alone cannot explain acquisition of inversion without cooperation of something in the subject himself, since many people subjected to same influences do not become inverted
A certain degree of anatomical hermaphroditism occurs normally, with every normal individual showing traces of the apparatus of the opposite sex
An originally bisexual physical disposition has in evolution become modified into a unisexual one, leaving behind traces of the atrophied sex

Inversion and somatic hermaphroditism are on the whole independent of each other - there is no close connection between hypothetical psychical hermaphroditism and established anatomical hermaphroditism
The most complete mental masculinity can be combined with inversion, showing that sexual object choice is independent of other mental characteristics

The popular view that the sexual instinct is absent in childhood and only awakens at puberty is not merely a simple error but one that has grave consequences, causing our present ignorance of fundamental sexual conditions
A thorough study of sexual manifestations of childhood would reveal the essential characters of the sexual instinct and show its development course and how it is assembled from various sources

The influence of childhood should be easier to understand and claim consideration before heredity in explaining adult characteristics, yet writers have devoted more attention to phylogenetic than ontogenetic primaeval periods
Not a single author has clearly recognized the regular existence of a sexual instinct in childhood, and the chapter on sexual development is as a rule omitted in writings on child development
Infantile amnesia - the peculiar amnesia which hides the earliest beginnings of childhood up to the sixth or eighth year - is responsible for neglect of infantile sexuality

The impressions of childhood that we have forgotten have left the deepest traces on our minds and had a determining effect upon our later development, representing repression rather than real abolition
Infantile amnesia provides a point of comparison between mental states of children and psychoneurotics, and may be brought into relation with the sexual impulses of childhood
Hysterical amnesia is only explicable by the subject already possessing memory-traces withdrawn from conscious disposal which are connected by an associative link to the forgotten material
During the latency period are built up the mental forces which later impede the sexual instinct - disgust, shame, and aesthetic and moral ideals - like dams restricting its flow
The construction of mental dams against sexuality is organically determined and fixed by heredity, and can occasionally occur without any help from education

These mental constructions emerge at the cost of infantile sexual impulses themselves, whose energy is diverted from sexual use and directed to other ends - a process deserving the name of sublimation
Powerful components are acquired for every kind of cultural achievement by diversion of sexual instinctual forces from sexual aims to new ones through sublimation

The sexual impulses of childhood cannot be utilized since reproductive functions are deferred, and these impulses are perverse - arising from erotogenic zones and arousing unpleasure, evoking opposing mental forces
Sexual activity makes a child ineducable, which is why educators stigmatize every sexual manifestation as a vice, though they cannot do much against it

Thumb-sucking (sensual sucking) consists in rhythmic repetition of sucking contact by the mouth with no purpose of taking nourishment, involving complete absorption of attention and often leading to a motor reaction like an orgasm
Many children proceed from thumb-sucking to masturbation, showing continuity between oral and genital sexuality
The most striking feature of infantile sexual activity is that the instinct is not directed towards other people but obtains satisfaction from the subject's own body - it is auto-erotic

The child's lips behave like an erotogenic zone, and stimulation by the warm flow of milk is the cause of pleasurable sensation - sexual activity first attaches itself to functions serving self-preservation
To begin with, sexual activity attaches itself to functions serving the purpose of self-preservation and does not become independent of them until later
The picture of a baby sinking back satiated from the breast with flushed cheeks and blissful smile persists as a prototype of the expression of sexual satisfaction in later life
The need for repeating sexual satisfaction becomes detached from the need for taking nourishment when teeth appear and food is chewed rather than only sucked

Constitutional intensification of the erotogenic significance of the labial region persisting into adulthood produces epicures in kissing, perverse kissing, powerful motives for drinking and smoking, or if repression ensues, disgust at food and hysterical vomiting

The three essential characteristics of infantile sexual manifestation are: (1) it attaches itself to vital somatic functions, (2) it has no sexual object and is thus auto-erotic, (3) its sexual aim is dominated by an erotogenic zone
An erotogenic zone is a part of the skin or mucous membrane in which stimuli of a certain sort evoke a feeling of pleasure possessing a particular quality

The anal zone shows that erotogenic zones are portions of the skin with heightened excitability innately present, and all erotogenic zones are used to provide a certain amount of pleasure obtained by stimulation according to some rhythm
The anal zone retains considerable susceptibility to genital stimulation throughout life, and intestinal disturbances in childhood have a determining influence on later neurotic symptoms
Children making use of the anal zone hold back their stool to produce violent muscular contractions and powerful pleasurable stimulation of the mucous membrane as it passes through the anus

The contents of the bowels are treated as part of the infant's own body, representing his first 'gift' - by producing them he expresses active compliance with environment, by withholding them his disobedience
From being a 'gift' feces later acquire the meaning of 'baby' - for babies, according to one sexual theory of children, are acquired by eating and born through the bowels

The prohibition against getting pleasure from anal activity and its products has a decisive effect on the child's whole development - this is the first occasion the infant glimpses an environment hostile to instinctual impulses and learns to separate his entity from the alien one, carrying out his first repression
What is 'anal' remains the symbol of everything to be repudiated and excluded from life after the first prohibition against anal pleasure

The genital zone is destined to great things in the future - early infantile masturbation, which scarcely a single individual escapes, establishes the foundations for the future primacy exercised by this erotogenic zone

Three phases of infantile masturbation are to be distinguished: the first belongs to early infancy, the second to the brief efflorescence of sexual activity about the fourth year of life, and only the third corresponds to pubertal masturbation
Positive and negative perversions can be traced back to a number of component instincts, which are not of a primary nature but are susceptible to further analysis

An instinct is provisionally understood as the psychical representative of an endosomatic, continuously flowing source of stimulation, contrasted with a stimulus set up by single excitations from without
The concept of instinct lies on the frontier between the mental and the physical, representing a measure of the demand made upon the mind for work
In itself an instinct is without quality - what distinguishes instincts from one another and endows them with specific qualities is their relation to their somatic sources and to their aims
The source of an instinct is a process of excitation occurring in an organ and the immediate aim of the instinct lies in the removal of this organic stimulus
Erotogenic zones behave in every respect like a portion of the sexual apparatus - in hysteria they become the seat of new sensations and changes in innervation comparable to genital erection
The eye corresponds to an erotogenic zone in scopophilia and exhibitionism, while the skin assumes this role in components of sexual instinct involving pain and cruelty
When libido fails to obtain satisfaction along normal lines, it behaves like a stream whose main bed has become blocked, proceeding to fill up collateral channels which may hitherto have been empty

Sexual repression as an internal factor must be put alongside external factors like limitation of freedom and inaccessibility of normal sexual objects, which bring about perversions in persons who might otherwise remain normal

Neurosis will always produce its greatest effects when constitution and experience work together in the same direction - what is innate and what is accidentally experienced are not opposing but cooperative factors
An unbroken chain bridges the gap between the neuroses in all their manifestations and normality - we are all to some extent hysterics
The extraordinarily wide dissemination of perversions forces us to suppose that the disposition to perversions is of no great rarity but must form a part of what passes as the normal constitution
There is something innate lying behind the perversions, but it is something innate in everyone as a disposition varying in intensity and increased by influences of actual life

What is innate are the constitutional roots of the sexual instinct - these may grow into actual vehicles of sexual activity (perversions), attract sexual energy as symptoms (neurosis), or through effective restriction bring about normal sexual life

The postulated constitution containing germs of all perversions will only be demonstrable in children - a formula takes shape that the sexuality of neurotics has remained in or been brought back to an infantile state

Every active perversion is accompanied by its passive counterpart in the unconscious - exhibitionists are simultaneously voyeurs, those with repressed sadistic impulses have masochistic determinants of symptoms

In any fairly marked case of psychoneurosis, a considerable number of perverse instincts are usually found, with traces of them all, though the degree of development of each is independent of the others

The aetiological factors of neurosis operate according to a complemental series, in which diminishing intensity of one factor (constitution) is balanced by increasing intensity of another (accidental experiences); extreme cases exist at both ends where one factor alone may be sufficient.

The single aetiological series must be divided into two subseries: the dispositional series (where constitution and childhood experiences interact) and the definitive series (where disposition and later traumatic experiences interact).
All factors that impair sexual development show their effects by bringing about regression - a return to an earlier phase of development.

Spontaneous sexual precocity can be demonstrated with certainty in the aetiology of neuroses, though it is not in itself a sufficient cause; it manifests in the interruption, abbreviation, or ending of the latency period.
Sexual precocity causes disturbances by occasioning sexual manifestations that, due to incomplete sexual inhibitions and undeveloped genital system, are bound to be in the nature of perversions.

Sexual precocity makes more difficult the later control of the sexual instinct by higher mental agencies and increases the impulsive quality characterizing the psychical representations of the instinct.

Sexual precocity often runs parallel with premature intellectual development; when linked in this way, it is found in the childhood history of persons of greatest eminence and capacity, and under such conditions its effects are not as pathogenic as when it appears in isolation.

The order in which various instinctual impulses come into activity is phylogenetically determined, as is the length of time they can manifest before succumbing to newly emerging impulses or typical repression.
Variations occur in both temporal sequence and duration of instinctual impulses, and these variations exercise a determining influence upon the final developmental outcome.

The effect of a repression cannot be undone; therefore, whether a given current appears earlier or later than an opposing current is not a matter of indifference, and divergences in temporal sequence invariably produce differences in outcome.

Instinctual impulses that emerge with special intensity often run a surprisingly short course (as seen in the heterosexual attachment of persons who later become manifest homosexuals); there is no justification for fearing that trends setting in violently in childhood will permanently dominate adult character.

Early sexual impressions are characterized by increased pertinacity or susceptibility to fixation in persons who later become neurotics or perverts; the same premature manifestations in others fail to make deep impressions and do not tend toward compulsive repetition.

The pertinacity of early impressions may be partially explained by the preponderance attaching in mental life to memory-traces in comparison with recent impressions; this factor is clearly dependent on intellectual education and increases in proportion to the degree of individual culture.

There exists an inverse relation between civilization and the free development of sexuality; consequently, the course of a child's sexual life is unimportant for later life where cultural level is low, but highly important where that level is high.

Accidental stimulations of infantile sexuality (especially seduction by other children or adults) provide material that, with the help of constitutional factors and pertinacity, can become fixated as permanent disorder.
A good proportion of deviations from normal sexual life observed in neurotics and perverts are established from the very first by impressions of childhood - a period regarded as devoid of sexuality.
The causation of sexual pathology is shared between compliant constitution, precocity, increased pertinacity of early impressions, and chance stimulation of sexual instinct by extraneous influences.

We know far too little of the biological processes constituting the essence of sexuality to construct from our fragmentary information a theory adequate to understanding both normal and pathological conditions.
Psycho-analysis is an outgrowth of the cathartic method discussed in Studies on Hysteria (1895); the cathartic therapy was Breuer's discovery, first used successfully ten years before the Studies.

The cathartic method presupposed that the patient could be hypnotized and was based on the widening of consciousness that occurs under hypnosis; its aim was symptom removal by inducing the patient to return to the psychical state in which the symptom first appeared.

The symptom represents a transformation ('conversion') of suppressed processes that have not reached consciousness; therapeutic effectiveness was due to discharge of 'strangulated' affect attaching to suppressed mental acts ('abreaction').

The cathartic method's therapeutic efficacy lies not in suggestion but in the expectation that symptoms will disappear automatically once the operation succeeds in diverting mental processes from their previous channel that found outlet in symptom formation.

Freud abandoned hypnosis while retaining the cathartic method's rejection of suggestion; patients lie comfortably on a sofa with the analyst sitting behind them outside their field of vision, proceeding like a conversation between two equally awake people.

Abandoning hypnosis assured applicability of treatment to unlimited numbers of patients, since many neurotic patients cannot be hypnotized by any means and hypnotizability depends on the patient's choice regardless of physician's skill.
Free associations - the involuntary thoughts ordinarily pushed aside as disturbing - serve as completely satisfactory substitute for the widening of consciousness that hypnosis provided.

The patient must 'let himself go' in what he says, including whatever comes into his head even if he thinks it unimportant, irrelevant, nonsensical, or embarrassing; he must not omit any thought because relating it would be distressing.

Gaps appear in the neurotic patient's memory during narration: actual occurrences are forgotten, chronological order is confused, causal connections are broken; no neurotic case history is without amnesia of some kind.

When the patient is urged to fill memory gaps, all ideas that occur are pushed back by every possible critical expedient until he feels positive discomfort when the memory really returns; this demonstrates that amnesias result from repression motivated by feelings of unpleasure.

The psychical forces that brought about repression can be detected in the 'resistance' that operates against recovery of lost memories; resistance is one of the corner-stones of psychoanalytic theory.
Ideas normally pushed aside are derivatives of repressed psychical phenomena (thoughts and impulses), distorted owing to resistance against their reproduction.

The greater the resistance, the greater the distortion; the relation between substitute idea and repressed material is brought to consciousness through interpretation, which constructs the repressed material from signs provided by the derivative.

Psychical material appears to be organized in multiple stratifications ordered according to themes; fragments of the same theme run through collections of memories with varying chronological distance, requiring zigzag movement through the patient's life history.

Most striking is the organization of pathogenic material into concentric stratifications around the pathogenic nucleus, ordered by degrees of resistance; the most peripheral strata contain memories easily remembered and clearly conscious, while approaching the nucleus encounters increasing resistance.
Memories composing the nucleus never become fully conscious; even after resolution of resistances, they can only be constructed and accepted by the patient with varying degrees of recognition.

Resistances must be recognized and pointed out to the patient; once a resistance is overcome, the next appears, so that following resistances systematically enables working through to the pathogenic nucleus.

The physician's task is like excavating a buried city: clearing away resistances in the material, then constructing from hints and remains what can never be fully brought to consciousness but must be accepted through intellectual conviction.
The work of overcoming resistances is the essential function distinguishing psycho-analysis from other suggestive procedures and constitutes its scientific character.

Theoretically, one might expect that overcoming all resistances would bring recovery; however, a complication arises from the tendency called 'transference' - the patient directing powerful emotional impulses toward the physician.

Transference can be positive (affectionate) or hostile; it arises spontaneously in all human relationships and especially in the patient-physician relation; in analysis, it becomes the most powerful instrument of resistance.

Initially, transference carries analysis forward with physician's authority; but when it becomes hostile or erotic, it becomes the most powerful resistance, making the patient suddenly lose understanding and interest in the work.

Transference must itself be uncovered and recognized; it originates from the same sources as earlier resistances and must be resolved by demonstration that such feelings are reproductions of old reactions.

When the patient's relation to the physician is straightened out, all obstacles to understanding and accepting repressed material disappear; psycho-analysis succeeds in solving the psychological problem of treatment that suggestion-based methods leave unsolved.

Psycho-analysis does not simply command the patient to overcome resistances but traces their origin and helps reduce them; the patient's residual resistance and degree of self-change achieve the therapeutic alteration sought.

The whole complex of influences determining recovery can be brought together under the single heading 'transference'; cure is achieved by suggestion, but suggestion means influencing someone by transferential phenomena.
Psycho-analytic independence of suggestion consists in transforming transference itself into the object of treatment and dissolving it by recognizing it as a repetition of earlier reactions.

Repression fails to suppress the instinct's capacity to become conscious; instead, it develops power to generate psychical derivatives and substitute connections that become symptoms - substitute satisfactions for what has been repressed.
Symptoms are substitute satisfactions that cause suffering either from their own nature or by bringing the person into conflict with their environment and social requirements.

Treatment induces the patient to give up repressions early in life and replace them with reactions corresponding to psychical maturity; this is accomplished through overcoming resistances by special art of interpretation.

Psycho-analysis demands continuous work by patient and physician over period of months, arousing unpopularity compared to shorter methods; but this very difficulty is used as new motive for combating the patient's illness.

Psycho-analysis is not applicable to persons lacking certain cultural and reliable character; indications include all chronic forms of hysteria and obsessions, and patients with pathological character traits, perversions, phobias.
Psycho-analysis may worsen acute hysteria or intensify confusional states in certain disorders; patients over fifty lack plasticity of mental processes necessary for therapy.

Normality results from repression of certain component instincts and infantile disposition constituents, with remaining constituents subordinated under genital zone primacy in service of the reproductive function.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Perversions correspond to disturbances of normal coalescence owing to overpowering and compulsive development of certain component instincts; neuroses can be traced to excessive repression of libidinal trends.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Almost all perverse instincts of infantile disposition can be recognized as forces concerned in symptom formation in neuroses, though in state of repression; neurosis is the 'negative' of perversion.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Two positions have never been repudiated or abandoned despite modifications: the importance of sexuality and of infantilism in aetiology of psychoneuroses.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Accidental influences have been replaced by constitutional factors, and 'defence' in purely psychological sense has been replaced by organic 'sexual repression' in the evolution of aetiological theory.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Psycho-analytic examination reveals that the patient's symptoms constitute his sexual activity (wholly or in part), arising from sources of normal or perverse component instincts of sexuality.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Large part of hysterical symptomatology derives directly from expressions of sexual excitement; erotogenic zones attain genital significance during neuroses through intensification of infantile characteristics.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Most complicated symptoms represent, through 'conversion,' phantasies having sexual situation as subject-matter; anyone who interprets hysteria's language recognizes the neurosis concerns only the patient's repressed sexuality.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Sexual function must be understood in its true extent as laid down by disposition in infancy; wherever commonplace emotion appears among determinants of illness onset, analysis invariably shows the sexual component of traumatic experience produced the pathogenic result.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: The essence of psychoneuroses lies in disturbances of sexual processes - the processes determining formation and utilization of sexual libido in the organism.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: These sexual processes are in the last resort of a chemical nature; 'actual' neuroses represent somatic effects of disturbances of sexual metabolism, while psychoneuroses represent psychical effects of those same disturbances.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: The similarity of neuroses to intoxication/abstinence phenomena and to Graves' disease and Addison's disease is clinically striking; like those diseases, neuroses may eventually be excluded from 'nervous diseases' category despite their name.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Aetiology of neuroses comprises everything that can act detrimentally upon processes serving sexual function; foremost are noxae affecting sexual function itself as judged injurious by sexual constitution varying with culture and education.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: A single pathogenic influence is scarcely ever sufficient; in large majority of cases, multiple aetiological factors are required that support one another and must not be regarded as mutually opposed.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Neurotic illness cannot be sharply differentiated from health; illness onset is product of summation, and the necessary total of aetiological determinants can be completed from any direction.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: To attribute aetiology of neuroses exclusively to heredity/constitution or solely to accidental influences on sexuality would be equally one-sided; better insight shows the essence lies in disturbance of organism's sexual processes.
source: SEXUALITY IN THE AETIOLOGY OF THE NEUROSES

quote: Foremost among psychical treatment measures is the use of words; words are the essential tool operating immediately upon the human mind, whether treating mental or physical disorders.

Words originally possessed magical power and retain much of ancient potency; through words one person can make another blessed or drive them to despair, transmit knowledge, and induce affective states.

The magical power of words is intelligible to psychologists: words evoke affects and are the universal means by which humans influence each other; despising word-use in psychotherapy means abandoning psychical influence.

Jokes have not received nearly as much philosophical consideration as they deserve given the part they play in mental life; only few thinkers (Jean Paul, Vischer, Fischer, Lipps) have entered deeply into problems of jokes.
source: JOKES AND THEIR RELATION TO THE UNCONSCIOUS

quote: It appears impracticable to deal with jokes otherwise than in connection with the comic; jokes represent something comic that is entirely subjective - produced by us, attached to our action.
source: JOKES AND THEIR RELATION TO THE UNCONSCIOUS

quote: A joke is generally defined as 'any conscious and successful evocation of what is comic, whether comic of observation or of situation.'
source: JOKES AND THEIR RELATION TO THE UNCONSCIOUS

quote: Fischer defines a joke as 'a playful judgement' - just as aesthetic freedom lies in playful contemplation of things, aesthetic attitude being characterized by not demanding satisfaction of serious needs but contenting ourselves with contemplative enjoyment.
source: JOKES AND THEIR RELATION TO THE UNCONSCIOUS
