quote: Transference arises spontaneously in all human relationships and in the patient's relations to the physician - it is the most powerful ally and the most formidable source of resistance in psycho-analytic treatment.

Each person develops a stereotyped method of conducting himself in sexual life (a 'psychical attitude' or 'transference pattern') through the combined operation of innate disposition and influences experienced in childhood, determining how he conducts himself in love throughout life.

If someone's need for love is not entirely satisfied by reality, he must direct his libidinal anticipatory ideas to every new person who appears, and it is highly probable that both portions of his libido (conscious and unconscious) participate in this attitude.

It is perfectly normal that the libido-cathexis of someone who is partly unsatisfied should be directed by anticipation to the person of the physician - this cathexis will attach itself to one of the 'imagos' or stereotyped patterns already present in the patient.

The physician becomes attached in the patient's mind to one of the 'imagos' of people by whom he was accustomed to be treated with affection (typically the father or mother imago), and under this assimilation the transference develops.

Transference in analysis is of two types: where it is of affectionate or friendly content that is admissible to consciousness, it becomes the vehicle of success and serves as the motive force of the joint work; but where it consists of unconscious erotic impulses, it becomes a resistance.
The struggle over transference-resistance constitutes the main work of the analysis - it is in this struggle that the patient must be convinced of the existence and power of his unconscious feelings.

The unconscious impulses do not want to be remembered as the treatment desires them to be, but endeavor to reproduce themselves in accordance with the timelessness of the unconscious and its capacity for hallucination - the patient seeks to act them out in the transference.
In severe cases, the unconscious impulses make use of the transference as their chief resistance to the treatment, transforming the most powerful ally into the greatest obstacle.
Transference is the battlefield on which all the contending forces must meet - it is in the handling of the transference that the fate of the analysis is decided.

The psycho-analyst should be opaque to his patients and, like a mirror, should show them nothing but what is shown to him - revealing nothing about himself and remaining a blank screen for projection.

The doctor should withhold all conscious influences from his capacity to attend and give himself over completely to his 'unconscious memory' - maintaining an attitude of 'evenly-suspended attention' to all that one hears.

The analyst should not direct his notice toward particular elements or make selections based on expectations or inclinations - as soon as attention is deliberately concentrated, one begins to select and follows inclination, which is precisely what must not be done.

The rule for the analyst is to be the counterpart to the fundamental rule of psycho-analysis for the patient: the patient should communicate everything without selection or omission; the analyst should catch everything with his 'evenly-suspended attention' without privileging anything.

The analyst's unconscious is able to reconstruct the patient's unconscious from the derivatives of the unconscious which are communicated to him - the analyst uses his own unconscious as an instrument to understand the patient's unconscious.

The analyst must not tolerate in himself any resistances which hold back from consciousness what has been perceived by his unconscious - if the analyst has not fully analyzed his own complexes and resistances, they will interfere with his perception of the patient's material.

Every analyst should begin his activity with a self-analysis and continually carry it deeper while making observations on his patients - anyone who fails to produce results in self-analysis should recognize his unfitness to treat patients by analysis.
The analyst should not take notes during sessions, as selection of material for notation interferes with evenly-suspended attention and introduces a disturbing element into the relationship.
The analyst must not attempt to remember particular material or fix his expectation on particular outcomes - memory works best when it functions spontaneously rather than being deliberately strained.

The analyst should not engage in scientific investigation during treatment - attempts to understand theoretically during the session interfere with therapeutic listening and introduce counter-transference elements.
No analyst can take a patient further than he has gone himself in his own analysis - the analyst's unresolved conflicts set limits to what he can accomplish with patients.
The analyst should avoid therapeutic ambition - the need to achieve cures or produce impressive results introduces personal motives that interfere with neutral analytic work.

Anyone who wishes to carry out analyses must conduct himself as emotionlessly as a surgeon performing an operation - the surgeon puts aside all his feelings including his human sympathy while focusing on the single aim of performing the operation as skillfully as possible.
Patients repeat in the transference all unwanted occurrences and painful emotions rather than remembering them as something belonging to the past - this is the essence of 'acting out' or 'agieren.'
The greater the resistance, the more extensively will acting out (repetition) replace remembering - in the most extreme cases, the patient does not remember anything at all but 'acts out' everything.

The patient reproduces material not as a memory but as an action - he repeats it without knowing that he is repeating it, demonstrating that the repressed seeks to be reproduced in present experience rather than remembered as past.

The transference creates an intermediate region between illness and real life through which the transition from one to the other is made - the transference is a piece of real experience but artificially constructed with provisional character.

The main instrument for curbing the patient's compulsion to repeat and transforming it into a motive for remembering is the handling of the transference - allowing repetition to unfold in the transference under controlled conditions permits analytic work on it.
Working-through the resistances is the part of the work that effects the greatest changes in the patient and distinguishes analytic treatment from any kind of treatment by suggestion.

Even after the patient has recognized a resistance and its interpretation, he must work through it - overcome it by continuing the analytic work under its dominance until it has been gradually exhausted.

The working-through of resistances is in practice an arduous task and a trial of patience for the patient and analyst alike, yet it is the part of the work that brings about the most enduring changes.

When the analyst perceives that the patient is experiencing an intense feeling of love for himself in the transference, this transference-love should neither be gratified nor suppressed, but should be handled as something unreal - an inevitable transference phenomenon to be traced back to its unconscious sources.

The patient's falling in love with the analyst is induced by the analytic situation and is not to be attributed to the charms of the analyst's own person - it has the character of a compulsion deriving from the patient's infantile fixations.

The patient's love in the transference is genuine and not less real for being transference - it has most of the qualities of genuine falling in love, though it is determined by infantile prototypes and lacks concern for reality.

The analyst must not yield to the patient's demands for love gratification, but equally must not brutally rebuff or express contempt for the patient's feelings - the analytic situation demands that both abstinence and respect be maintained.

If the analyst yielded to the patient's demands for love, it would be a victory for the patient but a complete defeat for the treatment - the analyst would have repeated the patient's infantile pattern without enabling any real insight or change.

The analyst's task is to keep the patient's transference-love in check and use it analytically - to show the patient that this love is determined by infantile fixations and to help trace it back to its unconscious roots.

The psychical apparatus is governed first by the pleasure principle - the automatic regulation by feelings belonging to the pleasure-unpleasure series, with unpleasure relating to an increase in excitation and pleasure to a diminution.
source: FORMULATIONS ON THE TWO PRINCIPLES OF MENTAL FUNCTIONING

quote: The pleasure principle proves inefficacious for the ego's self-preservation in the external world, as it seeks immediate gratification without regard for reality - a state of merely wishing cannot be sustained, as needs persist and become urgent.
source: FORMULATIONS ON THE TWO PRINCIPLES OF MENTAL FUNCTIONING

quote: The reality principle represents a modification of the pleasure principle - what is presented in the mind is no longer what is agreeable but what is real, even if it should be disagreeable, securing eventual pleasure through detour rather than immediate gratification.
source: FORMULATIONS ON THE TWO PRINCIPLES OF MENTAL FUNCTIONING

quote: With the introduction of the reality principle, one species of thought-activity was split off - phantasy remains dominated by the pleasure principle alone and continues seeking immediate wish-fulfillment free from reality-testing.
source: FORMULATIONS ON THE TWO PRINCIPLES OF MENTAL FUNCTIONING

quote: The replacement of the pleasure principle by the reality principle brings about a series of adaptations: development of attention, notation and memory, judgment as reality-testing, thought as experimental action, and motor discharge delayed for purposive action.
source: FORMULATIONS ON THE TWO PRINCIPLES OF MENTAL FUNCTIONING

quote: The supersession of the pleasure principle by the reality principle is not sudden or universal but affects different mental impulses to differing degrees - the sexual instincts remain governed by the pleasure principle for much longer.
source: FORMULATIONS ON THE TWO PRINCIPLES OF MENTAL FUNCTIONING

quote: Education can be described as an incitement to the conquest of the pleasure principle and its replacement by the reality principle - it seeks the individual's benefit and accelerates a developmental transition that would otherwise occur spontaneously.
source: FORMULATIONS ON THE TWO PRINCIPLES OF MENTAL FUNCTIONING

quote: Art represents a reconciliation of the two principles in a unique way - the artist turns from reality toward phantasy, but finds a way back by shaping phantasies into new realities that gain social value.
source: FORMULATIONS ON THE TWO PRINCIPLES OF MENTAL FUNCTIONING

quote: The replacement of the pleasure principle by the reality principle does not dethrone the former but only safeguards it - a momentary pleasure with uncertain results is given up, but only to gain a later assured pleasure through regard for reality.
source: FORMULATIONS ON THE TWO PRINCIPLES OF MENTAL FUNCTIONING

quote: Neurosis onset can occur through frustration - when reality refuses satisfaction of sexual needs, driving the libido to regressive paths of satisfaction and symptom-formation.

Neurosis can onset following developmental changes requiring increased libidinal demands beyond the person's capacity (as at puberty, menopause, or at critical life transitions), representing onset through excess demand.
Neurosis onset through inhibition of development occurs when the subject remains arrested at an earlier stage of psychosexual development and cannot advance to more mature forms of satisfaction.
Traumatic neuroses onset when a single powerful experience overwhelms the ego's capacity for mastery, breaking through stimulus protection and creating lasting disturbance.

The common factor in all types of neurosis onset is the inability of the ego to master the quantity of excitation confronting it, whether from external frustration, developmental demands, fixation, or trauma.
The disposition to obsessional neurosis lies in a pre-genital organization of the libido, in which sadism and anal erotism play the principal parts.
In obsessional neurosis, there has been a regression from the genital stage to the earlier sadistic-anal stage, transforming loving impulses into hostile ones and sexual aims into destructive ones.

The chronological sequence of libidinal development (oral, sadistic-anal, genital) suggests that obsessional neurosis requires fixation to have occurred at the sadistic-anal stage before the genital organization was reached.

The ambivalence characteristic of obsessional neurosis originates in the sadistic-anal phase where love and hate, tenderness and aggressivity, are not yet differentiated and coexist in the same impulse.

The temporal relation between the onset of the ego's repressive activity and the level of libidinal organization determines the choice of neurosis - premature ego development relative to libido development predisposes to obsessional neurosis.
In savage races, the first system of human thought (animism) assumes that everything in nature is animate like the self and populated by spirits - the world is conceived on the model of the ego.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - ANIMISM, MAGIC AND THE OMNIPOTENCE OF THOUGHTS

quote: Animism represents a psychological system organizing not only natural phenomena but also the technique for dealing with them - through magic, which assumes that physical processes follow the laws of mental life.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - ANIMISM, MAGIC AND THE OMNIPOTENCE OF THOUGHTS

quote: Magic operates by two principles: the law of similarity (like produces like, effects resemble causes) and the law of contact or contagion (things once in contact continue to act on each other at a distance).
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - ANIMISM, MAGIC AND THE OMNIPOTENCE OF THOUGHTS

quote: The omnipotence of thoughts is the primitive belief that wishes and thoughts can directly alter reality - the overvaluation of mental acts as if they were equivalent to real acts.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - ANIMISM, MAGIC AND THE OMNIPOTENCE OF THOUGHTS

quote: The omnipotence of thoughts persists in obsessional neurosis, where patients believe their hostile wishes can cause real harm and must be undone by compulsive rituals - demonstrating that this archaic mode returns in neurosis.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - ANIMISM, MAGIC AND THE OMNIPOTENCE OF THOUGHTS

quote: The development of human world-views follows three stages: the animistic (mythological), the religious, and the scientific, corresponding to the stages of libidinal development - narcissistic, object-finding, and maturity.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - ANIMISM, MAGIC AND THE OMNIPOTENCE OF THOUGHTS

quote: The taboo is the oldest form of human moral restriction, predating religion and having two opposed meanings: sacred/consecrated and uncanny/dangerous/forbidden/unclean.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - TABOO AND EMOTIONAL AMBIVALENCE

quote: Taboo prohibitions are different from religious or moral prohibitions - they have no foundation in divine command but impose their own authority, with violation producing automatic punishment through internal necessity.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - TABOO AND EMOTIONAL AMBIVALENCE

quote: The most ancient and important taboo prohibitions are the two fundamental laws of totemism: not to kill the totem animal and to avoid sexual intercourse with members of the totem clan of the opposite sex.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE HORROR OF INCEST

quote: The exogamy connected with the totem represents the most drastic measure preventing incest that could possibly have been devised - it prevents not only sexual intercourse with mother and sisters but with all female members of the clan.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE HORROR OF INCEST

quote: The incest taboo must have originated from a powerful incestuous desire - what is not desired does not need to be forbidden with such intensity and universality.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE HORROR OF INCEST

quote: The ambivalence toward the tabooed object is fundamental - the taboo person or thing is simultaneously desired and feared, attractive and dangerous, generating opposed emotional attitudes.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - TABOO AND EMOTIONAL AMBIVALENCE

quote: Taboo observances in primitive peoples are remarkably similar to obsessional symptoms in neurotics - both involve prohibitions, renunciations, isolation, ceremonials, and the fear that violation will be magically punished.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - TABOO AND EMOTIONAL AMBIVALENCE

quote: The psychological basis of taboo is ambivalent emotional attitudes - every taboo conceals a desire, and the prohibition protects against acting on the unconscious wish.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - TABOO AND EMOTIONAL AMBIVALENCE

quote: The treatment of enemies in primitive cultures reveals that they are simultaneously feared and revered - the taboos surrounding conquered enemies show that the victor both desires to harm and fears retaliation.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - TABOO AND EMOTIONAL AMBIVALENCE

quote: Rulers are subject to elaborate taboos because their subjects harbor both veneration and hostile impulses toward them - the taboo protects the ruler but also restricts him, expressing the ambivalence of the subject's attitude.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - TABOO AND EMOTIONAL AMBIVALENCE

quote: The taboo of the dead arises from ambivalence - survivors both mourn and harbor hostile feelings toward the deceased, and project these hostile wishes onto the ghost or spirit, which is then feared.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - TABOO AND EMOTIONAL AMBIVALENCE

quote: Conscience originates from ambivalence toward loved persons - the need to restrict hostile impulses toward those we love forms the basis of moral conscience and taboo observance.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - TABOO AND EMOTIONAL AMBIVALENCE

quote: The totem animal is a substitute for the father - totemism represents an early form of father-worship where the father's authority is projected onto an animal ancestor.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: The origin of totemism lies in the primal crime - in the remote past, the primal father was killed and eaten by his sons, who banded together against the father's monopoly over women and his tyrannical rule.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: After the parricide, the sons experienced remorse and guilt - they both hated the father who stood as an obstacle to their power and sexual wishes, but also loved and admired him.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: The taboo against killing the totem (father-substitute) and the prohibition of incest (renunciation of the women) represent deferred obedience to the father - the sons grant to the father in death the authority they had fought against in life.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: The totem meal, in which the totem animal is ceremonially killed and eaten by all clan members, represents a repetition and commemoration of the original parricide - a reenactment of the primal crime.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: By eating the father (totem animal), the sons achieve identification with him and acquire a portion of his strength - the totemic system is based on preserving the memory of the father through periodic renewal of the crime.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: Religion originates in the son's guilt over parricide - the totem animal is replaced by a god, and the son's ambivalence finds expression in the alternation of defiance and submission toward the deity.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: The Christian communion, in which believers consume the body and blood of Christ, represents the direct heir of the totem meal - it repeats the incorporation of the father-god to achieve union with him.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: Social organization originates in the brothers' renunciation of the women they had liberated by killing the father - to prevent fighting among themselves, they established exogamy and restricted each other's access to incestuous objects.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: The sense of guilt arising from the primal parricide has been inherited through phylogeny and persists in humanity's unconscious - it forms the basis of religion, morality, and social organization.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: The Oedipus complex is the nucleus of neurosis because it recapitulates humanity's primal experience - the child's wishes to kill the father and possess the mother repeat the original crime that founded human civilization.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: Children's animal phobias represent a displacement of fear and ambivalence from the father onto an animal substitute - the phobic animal is always a father-surrogate.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: The development of culture rests on restrictions placed on instinctual satisfaction - the renunciations imposed by totemism (no parricide, no incest) form the foundation of all subsequent moral and social development.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: Psycho-analysis has demonstrated that the first impulses of a child are incestuous and that such wishes persist in the unconscious throughout life, forming the nucleus of later neurotic conflict.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE HORROR OF INCEST

quote: The individual's emotional attitude toward the totem recapitulates the child's emotional attitude toward the father - the ambivalence, fear, and veneration directed toward the totem mirror the Oedipus complex.
source: TOTEM AND TABOO (1913) - THE RETURN OF TOTEMISM IN CHILDHOOD

quote: In the theme of the three caskets (Merchant of Venice, King Lear, Judgment of Paris), the choice among three women represents the choice among three essential forms of woman in man's life: the mother who bears him, the beloved who is his equal, and death who receives him.
source: THE THEME OF THE THREE CASKETS

quote: The third casket (lead) represents death - it is chosen because it symbolizes silence, pallor, and muteness, all attributes of death. The reversal in the myths (choosing the 'best' rather than 'death') represents wish-fulfillment against the reality of mortality.
source: THE THEME OF THE THREE CASKETS

quote: The three Fates (Moirae) - Clotho who spins the thread of life, Lachesis who measures it, and Atropos who cuts it - represent the three aspects of the mother: creator, maintainer, and destroyer.
source: THE THEME OF THE THREE CASKETS

quote: The Goddess of Death in mythology is derived from the Goddess of Love - the Greeks' Aphrodite and Romans' Venus originally possessed attributes of death deities, showing the connection between love and death.
source: THE THEME OF THE THREE CASKETS

quote: Man's relationship with the three women recapitulates his relationship with the mother throughout life - she is the woman who bears him (mother), the woman who loves him (beloved replacing the mother), and Mother Earth who receives him in death.
source: THE THEME OF THE THREE CASKETS

quote: The transformation in myths from terrifying Death to desirable Love represents the operation of wish-fulfillment - the myth replaces what we fear (death) with what we desire (love), allowing us to contemplate mortality.
source: THE THEME OF THE THREE CASKETS

quote: Fausse reconnaissance ('déjà raconté') - the feeling of having already told something that one is currently relating - represents a compromise formation between the impulse to tell something important from the past and the resistance against revealing it.

The 'déjà raconté' experience allows the person to enjoy the satisfaction of having revealed the important material (as though it had been told) while maintaining the repression (since it actually remains untold).

The Moses of Michelangelo represents Moses after his return from Sinai, in the moment of checking his anger at seeing the people worshiping the golden calf - the statue captures the instant of controlling intense affect.

Michelangelo modified the traditional Moses by depicting him restraining his anger rather than giving way to it - the statue shows a Moses who has overcome passion in favor of duty, representing the artist's own psychological conflict.

The meaning of great works of art can be discovered through minute observation of small details and features - trivial details often betray the creator's unconscious intentions more clearly than obvious features.
Art works affect us powerfully by arousing the same emotional state in us that existed in the artist during creation - we reconstruct the artist's psychological process from studying the work itself.
Dreams commonly incorporate material from fairy tales and myths, which serve as collective repositories of typical human experiences and unconscious phantasies shared across cultures.
source: THE OCCURRENCE IN DREAMS OF MATERIAL FROM FAIRY TALES

quote: Fairy tales and myths provide ready-made symbolic expressions for unconscious phantasies - the dreamer borrows from this cultural storehouse to express personal unconscious material.
source: THE OCCURRENCE IN DREAMS OF MATERIAL FROM FAIRY TALES

quote: The recurrence of the same themes in fairy tales across different cultures demonstrates that these tales express universal unconscious phantasies and typical human experiences.
source: THE OCCURRENCE IN DREAMS OF MATERIAL FROM FAIRY TALES

quote: Children's lies can serve defensive purposes - the child may lie to protect himself from punishment or to deny painful reality, and such lies should be understood as defensive operations rather than simple moral failures.
Some children's lies represent phantasy formations that have broken through into consciousness and been asserted as reality - the child confuses wish-fulfillment phantasy with actual events.
Overly harsh moral demands and punishment for lying may intensify rather than cure the tendency to lie - the child needs understanding of the phantasy behind the lie rather than pure condemnation.

In masturbation, both physical and psychological factors play roles - the act may serve to discharge sexual tension but also represents phantasy fulfillment and may become a vehicle for expressing unconscious wishes.
source: CONTRIBUTIONS TO A DISCUSSION ON MASTURBATION

quote: The harm in masturbation lies not in the physical act itself but in the excessive sense of guilt it produces, which may interfere with later sexual development and contribute to neurotic disposition.
source: CONTRIBUTIONS TO A DISCUSSION ON MASTURBATION

quote: Psycho-analysis claims interest from philosophy because it compels philosophy to take account of unconscious mental processes - any theory of mind that neglects the unconscious is inadequate.
source: THE CLAIMS OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS TO SCIENTIFIC INTEREST

quote: For philology and the study of language, psycho-analysis reveals that linguistic symbols and expressions often preserve ancient meanings connected to the body and sexual functions, with symbolism being rooted in unconscious equation of disparate objects.
source: THE CLAIMS OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS TO SCIENTIFIC INTEREST

quote: Psycho-analysis has interest for biology in demonstrating the fundamental bisexuality of human beings - both physical and psychological bisexual tendencies are present and influence sexual development and object-choice.
source: THE CLAIMS OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS TO SCIENTIFIC INTEREST

quote: For the history of civilization, psycho-analysis shows that the development of culture depends upon restriction of instinctual satisfaction - cultural progress requires instinctual renunciation and sublimation.
source: THE CLAIMS OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS TO SCIENTIFIC INTEREST

quote: In the study of religion, psycho-analysis demonstrates that religious beliefs and practices derive from the same sources as neurotic symptoms - both represent attempts to master infantile conflicts, particularly those relating to the father.
source: THE CLAIMS OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS TO SCIENTIFIC INTEREST

quote: Psycho-analysis shows that mythology and folklore preserve collective unconscious phantasies - myths represent disguised wish-fulfillments of whole peoples, just as dreams represent wish-fulfillments of individuals.
source: THE CLAIMS OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS TO SCIENTIFIC INTEREST

quote: For aesthetics, psycho-analysis reveals that artistic creation involves regression to primary process thinking combined with masterful form - the artist reconciles infantile pleasure sources with mature technique.
source: THE CLAIMS OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS TO SCIENTIFIC INTEREST

quote: In education, psycho-analysis shows that early childhood experiences have lasting effects on character formation - education should aim at optimal management of instinctual forces rather than mere suppression.
source: THE CLAIMS OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS TO SCIENTIFIC INTEREST

quote: Psycho-analysis demonstrates to sociology that individual psychology and group psychology are fundamentally connected - social institutions arise from attempts to solve common psychological problems.
source: THE CLAIMS OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS TO SCIENTIFIC INTEREST

quote: For jurisprudence, psycho-analysis shows that the concept of responsibility requires consideration of unconscious motivation - legal responsibility cannot be determined without understanding the unconscious forces behind actions.
source: THE CLAIMS OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS TO SCIENTIFIC INTEREST

quote: In psychiatry, psycho-analysis has shown that mental disorders previously thought meaningless have psychological meaning - every symptom serves a purpose in the patient's mental economy.
source: THE CLAIMS OF PSYCHO-ANALYSIS TO SCIENTIFIC INTEREST

quote: The position in which a person wakes from a dream often relates to the content of the dream and may represent a bodily expression of what was dreamed.
source: OBSERVATIONS AND EXAMPLES FROM ANALYTIC PRACTICE

quote: Self-criticism by neurotics often represents an internalized critical voice (superego precursor) directed against forbidden instinctual wishes - the patient attacks himself to avoid punishment from outside.
source: OBSERVATIONS AND EXAMPLES FROM ANALYTIC PRACTICE

quote: Dreams may condense or expand the representation of time - years may be represented as moments or moments as extended periods, reflecting the timelessness of unconscious processes.
source: OBSERVATIONS AND EXAMPLES FROM ANALYTIC PRACTICE

quote: Two rooms in a dream often represent the two openings of the female body (mouth and genitals) or the transformation from 'two' (breasts, buttocks) to 'one' (genitals), expressing sexual symbolism through spatial representation.
source: OBSERVATIONS AND EXAMPLES FROM ANALYTIC PRACTICE

quote: Fragmentary dreams that seem incomplete often defend against disturbing material by breaking off the dream narrative before reaching the anxiety-producing conclusion.
source: OBSERVATIONS AND EXAMPLES FROM ANALYTIC PRACTICE

quote: Dreams about dead people often fulfill wishes about their death or express guilt over having wished for their death - the dreamer's relationship with the deceased is re-experienced in the dream.
source: OBSERVATIONS AND EXAMPLES FROM ANALYTIC PRACTICE

quote: When symptoms of the illness appear in the dream, this often indicates that the symptoms are being used as day-residues to express unconscious wish-fulfillment - the symptom becomes material for dream-work.
source: OBSERVATIONS AND EXAMPLES FROM ANALYTIC PRACTICE

quote: Overcoats in dreams typically symbolize condoms - the dream expresses sexual wishes or anxieties about sexual intercourse through the overcoat representation.
source: OBSERVATIONS AND EXAMPLES FROM ANALYTIC PRACTICE

quote: Shoes in dreams commonly represent the female genitals - dirty, torn, or disgraced shoes (feet) express devaluation or disgust toward sexuality.
source: OBSERVATIONS AND EXAMPLES FROM ANALYTIC PRACTICE

quote: The representation of ages in dreams often condenses different time periods - a person may appear simultaneously at different ages, or one person's age may represent another's, expressing temporal displacement.
source: OBSERVATIONS AND EXAMPLES FROM ANALYTIC PRACTICE

quote: Dreams with an unrecognized precipitating cause often conceal distressing current events that the dreamer wishes to deny - the manifest dream obscures its connection to recent trauma.
source: OBSERVATIONS AND EXAMPLES FROM ANALYTIC PRACTICE

quote: In folklore dreams, particularly those about bodily functions and sexuality, the dream-symbolism reveals unconscious equations between disparate bodily products and sexual elements that are shared across cultures.
Penis symbolism appears commonly in folklore dreams through representations of elongated objects, tools, and weapons - the symbolic equation is evident across different cultural traditions.
Faeces symbolism in dreams and folklore equates excrement with gold, money, and gifts - the unconscious preserves the infantile equation between bodily products and valuable possessions.
Dreams about finding treasure typically represent wish-fulfillments related to anal erotism and the discovery of one's own body - the treasure symbolizes valuable bodily products or sexual organs.
The ring in dreams and folklore commonly symbolizes the female genitals - stories about magic rings or rings of fidelity express unconscious phantasies about female sexuality.

Educational experiences in adolescence (particularly at gymnasium/secondary school) leave lasting impressions because they occur during a time of heightened emotional receptivity and when the personality is being formed.
Teachers inevitably serve as father-substitutes for their students - the student's emotional relationship with teachers recapitulates and is determined by his relationship with his father.

Students transfer onto their teachers the ambivalent feelings they harbor toward their fathers - they both admire and rebel against teachers with the same ambivalence that characterizes the father-son relationship.

The liberation from parental authority is one of the most necessary but also most painful achievements of development - education aids this process by providing other authority figures (teachers) through whom the conflict with the father can be partially worked through.
Former students' idealization of certain teachers and scorn for others reflects not the objective merits of these teachers but the students' own emotional needs and conflicts projected onto them.

The significant psychological influence of teachers derives not from their knowledge but from their personalities - students are affected by the teacher as a person rather than by the subject matter taught.

School experiences gain their significance from the fact that they represent the student's first major encounter with social organization outside the family - the school is the training ground for adult social life.

Students' ambivalent feelings toward their teachers protect them from being overwhelmed by exclusive attachment to their fathers - the distribution of ambivalence across multiple authority figures is developmentally beneficial.

The psycho-analytic view of psychogenic disturbance demonstrates that conversion symptoms occur when the ego-instincts are overwhelmed by sexual instincts - the organ 'struck with repression' becomes unable to fulfill its ego-function.
source: THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC VIEW OF PSYCHOGENIC DISTURBANCE OF VISION

quote: The selection of the organ for conversion symptom is not arbitrary but determined by the specific role that organ plays in both ego-preservation and sexual gratification - the organ must serve both instinctual groups.
source: THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC VIEW OF PSYCHOGENIC DISTURBANCE OF VISION

quote: The relation of the eye to objects combines both functions - perception for self-preservation and scopophilic sexual pleasure - which is why it becomes a site for conversion symptoms when these functions conflict.
source: THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC VIEW OF PSYCHOGENIC DISTURBANCE OF VISION

quote: In Schreber's delusional system, his transformation into God's wife and the subsequent redemption of the world through bearing divine offspring represents the ultimate fulfillment of his feminine homosexual wish toward his father, sublimated into religious-cosmic significance.

The voices (Stimmenhören) experienced in paranoia and related conditions represent the auditory perception of thoughts - what should remain internal mental processes break through into consciousness as external voices.

Schreber's concept of 'soul murder' (Seelenmord) represents his unconscious perception of the psychological danger posed by submission to homosexual wishes - the fear that yielding to these wishes would destroy his masculine identity.

The successful adaptation to reality in paranoia's reconstruction phase should not be mistaken for recovery - the delusional system represents a new pathological equilibrium rather than restoration of normal functioning.
Psycho-analysis is Freud's creation and remains fundamentally associated with his name, distinguishing it from the theories of Adler and Jung which are not properly psycho-analytic
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: The theory of repression is the cornerstone on which the whole structure of psycho-analysis rests, and anyone who cannot accept repression cannot belong to psychoanalysis
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: The theory of resistance and repression, of the unconscious, of the aetiological significance of sexual life and of the importance of infantile experiences constitute the principal subject-matter of psycho-analysis and the foundations of its theory
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: Adler's theory replaces sexual motivation with the 'masculine protest' and 'neurotic character', thereby abandoning the foundations of psycho-analysis while claiming to improve it
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: Jung's modifications dissolve the findings of psycho-analysis by reinterpreting the Oedipus complex symbolically rather than literally, thereby making it a mere figure of speech rather than an actual childhood experience
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: Jung's denial of infantile sexuality and his recasting of libido as general psychic energy represent a complete abandonment of psychoanalytic principles, making his system incompatible with psycho-analysis
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: The history of psycho-analysis falls into two phases: in the first, Freud worked alone; in the second, after 1907, the movement expanded internationally with collaborators who both advanced and threatened the integrity of the theory
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: Psycho-analysis necessarily arouses resistances because it challenges narcissism by showing that the ego is not master in its own house, following earlier blows to human narcissism from Copernicus and Darwin
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: The technique of free association, where patients report everything that comes to mind without selection, is the fundamental method of psycho-analytic investigation, replacing hypnosis
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: Dream-interpretation is the via regia (royal road) to knowledge of the unconscious and remains the most secure foundation of psycho-analysis
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: Parapraxes (slips of the tongue, pen, forgetting, bungled actions, etc.) are not mere accidents or chance events but have psychological meaning and arise from the interference of two different intentions
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE II-III

quote: In slips of the tongue, an intention interferes with another intention, producing a compromise formation where both intentions are partially expressed in the resultant speech error
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE II

quote: The disturbing intention in parapraxes may be a direct contrary intention (saying the opposite of what is intended) or may be entirely unrelated content that intrudes from another psychological complex
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE II

quote: The forgetting of intentions (forgetting to do something) regularly derives from a counter-will or contrary intention, though the person is typically unaware of this opposing motive
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE III

quote: The forgetting of names often occurs when the name is associatively connected to material that the person wishes to avoid or that arouses painful affect, even when the connection is indirect or symbolic
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE III

quote: Losing or mislaying objects is frequently a sacrifice to Fate, an unconscious attempt to ward off feared losses by voluntarily sacrificing something of lesser importance
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE III

quote: Bungled actions that appear to be mere clumsiness often fulfill wishes that should be denied, with the intention disguising itself as a lucky accident
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE III

quote: The study of parapraxes demonstrates that there are mental purposes or intentions operating in people without their conscious knowledge, forcing us to accept the hypothesis of unconscious mental processes
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE IV

quote: Superstitious people who believe in chance and omens are correct in sensing that external events have meaning, but they project outward what psycho-analysis finds inward—that seemingly chance psychical events have internal psychological causation
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE IV

quote: The apparently arbitrary choice of numbers that someone 'freely' selects is actually determined by unconscious thoughts and associations, proving that even seemingly random mental acts follow deterministic laws
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE IV

quote: Dreams are not somatic but psychical phenomena, the products of the dreamer's own mental activity with sense and meaning that can be uncovered through psycho-analytic interpretation
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE V

quote: The manifest content of the dream (what the dreamer remembers and reports) differs from the latent dream-thoughts (the unconscious meaning), and the dream-work transforms the latter into the former through specific mechanisms
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE VI

quote: The technique of dream interpretation requires the dreamer to free-associate to each element of the manifest dream without criticism, allowing the latent thoughts to emerge through these associations
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE VI

quote: Dreams regularly fulfill wishes, though the wish may be disguised or distorted; even anxiety dreams and punishment dreams represent wish-fulfillments, though of a complex kind
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE VIII

quote: The dream-censorship is a psychical force that distorts the latent dream-thoughts to make them acceptable to consciousness, operating like a political censorship that modifies communications to make them permissible
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE IX

quote: Dream-distortion increases with the intensity of censorship; transparent dreams occur when censorship is weak (as in children or when waking wishes align with unconscious wishes), while highly distorted dreams indicate strong censorship
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE IX

quote: Dream-symbolism represents a primitive, archaic mode of expression where certain manifest elements regularly stand for particular latent contents (e.g., houses for the human body, rooms for women, climbing for sexual intercourse)
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURES X-XI

quote: The majority of dream-symbols are sexual in nature, with an overwhelming preponderance of symbols representing the male genital organ, female genitals, and the sexual act itself
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURES X-XI

quote: Dream-symbols are not peculiar to dreams but appear in myths, fairy-tales, jokes, folklore, and everyday speech, suggesting a universal symbolic language rooted in phylogenetic inheritance
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XI

quote: The dream-work employs four main mechanisms: condensation (multiple latent elements compressed into single manifest element), displacement (psychical intensity transferred from important to trivial elements), considerations of representability (abstract thoughts converted to visual images), and secondary revision (logical facade imposed post-hoc)
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURES XI-XIV

quote: Condensation in dreams allows a single manifest element to represent multiple latent thoughts simultaneously, creating the remarkable compression that makes manifest dreams much shorter than latent content
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XII

quote: Displacement in dreams transfers psychical value from important elements to unimportant ones, making what is trivial prominent and what is significant peripheral, thereby evading censorship
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XII

quote: The requirement of representability forces dream-thoughts to be translated into predominantly visual images, favoring concrete over abstract content and creating the hallucinatory quality of dreams
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XIII

quote: Secondary revision attempts to impose logical coherence and narrative structure on the incoherent dream material after the fact, creating the false impression that dreams are more organized than they actually are
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XIII

quote: Dreams invariably include a day's residue—recent impressions from waking life—but these are never the true instigator of the dream; rather, they provide material through which unconscious infantile wishes achieve expression
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XIV

quote: Every dream is instigated by an unconscious infantile wish that seeks fulfillment; adult rational wishes alone cannot produce dreams but must connect with these deeper infantile sources
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XIV

quote: Dreams perform the function of preserving sleep by converting disturbing stimuli (internal or external) into dream images that allow sleep to continue, making the dream the guardian of sleep
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XIV

quote: Anxiety dreams do not contradict wish-fulfillment theory; they occur when censorship fails partially, allowing repressed wishes to emerge with sufficient clarity to provoke anxiety, or when the ego fulfills its own wish to punish forbidden desires
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XIV

quote: Punishment dreams represent the fulfillment of a wish on the part of the censoring agency (superego) to punish the person for harboring forbidden wishes, thus still conforming to wish-fulfillment theory
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XIV

quote: The archaic, regressive character of dreams shows that dreaming reverts to earlier modes of mental functioning, employing primary process mechanisms and primitive forms of thought characteristic of childhood and unconscious mental life
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XV

quote: Symptoms, like dreams and parapraxes, are meaningful psychical products that can be interpreted; they represent compromise formations between repressed wishes and the forces of repression
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XVII

quote: Neurotic symptoms always have sense and meaning relating to the patient's life experiences; they are substitutes for satisfactions that have been renounced in reality
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XVII

quote: The fixation of libido at early developmental points creates predispositions to neurosis; when libido is frustrated in adult life, it regresses to these fixation points, producing neurotic symptoms
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XVIII-XIX

quote: Sexual development proceeds through a series of stages (oral, anal, phallic) before reaching the genital stage, with each stage capable of becoming a fixation point to which libido may later regress
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURES XIX-XX

quote: Infantile sexuality is characterized by component instincts (oral, anal, sadistic, scopophilic, exhibitionistic) that only gradually become organized under genital primacy; neurosis represents a failure of this organization
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XX

quote: The Oedipus complex—the child's erotic attachment to the parent of the opposite sex and hostile attitude toward the same-sex parent—is universal and forms the nuclear complex of the neuroses
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXI

quote: The aetiology of neurosis requires multiple factors working in a 'complemental series': hereditary constitutional factors, infantile experiences producing fixations, and precipitating adult frustrations that trigger regression
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXII

quote: Neurosis results from frustration (Versagung) when the libido is denied satisfaction in reality, but not all frustration produces neurosis—only when no adequate substitute satisfaction can be found and when ego-libido conflict develops
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXII

quote: The libido that is frustrated in reality flows backward (regresses) to earlier fixation points, reactivating infantile wishes and modes of satisfaction that then become the source of symptom formation
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXII-XXIII

quote: Symptoms arise from conflict between ego instincts (self-preservative) and sexual instincts (libido); the ego represses unacceptable libidinal demands, but the repressed libido returns in the form of symptoms
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXIII

quote: The pleasure principle governs unconscious mental processes, demanding immediate satisfaction, while the reality principle, developed by the ego, requires postponement and modification of satisfaction according to external conditions
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXIII

quote: Phantasy (imagination) represents a domain where the pleasure principle continues to reign unchallenged, providing substitute satisfactions and serving as the bridge between unfulfilled wishes and symptoms
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXIII

quote: Symptoms have two sides: they provide substitute sexual satisfaction for repressed libido (pleasure gain), but they also involve suffering that satisfies the need for punishment (guilt)
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXIV

quote: Common nervous states (neurasthenia, anxiety neurosis) differ from psychoneuroses in that their symptoms are directly produced by disturbed sexual physiology (actual neuroses) rather than being psychical products requiring interpretation
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXIV

quote: Anxiety in neurosis arises from undischarged libido that has been transformed directly into anxiety—libido that cannot find adequate release is converted into the affect of anxiety
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXV

quote: There are three types of anxiety: realistic anxiety (fear of external danger), neurotic anxiety (fear of one's own libido), and moral anxiety (fear of the superego/conscience)
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXV

quote: Symptoms have the character of a 'secondary gain from illness' beyond their primary function; they become integrated into the ego and serve various purposes (excuse for failure, means of dominating others, self-punishment), making the patient resist giving them up
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXIV

quote: Libido-theory distinguishes between ego-libido (narcissistic libido directed toward the self) and object-libido (directed toward external objects), with the two in inverse relationship
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXVI

quote: Transference occurs in all human relationships as stereotyped editions of emotional impulses and reactions from childhood, but in analysis it becomes concentrated on the analyst and must be recognized and interpreted
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXVII

quote: Transference can manifest as positive (affectionate, loving) or negative (hostile, antagonistic); both forms must be recognized and worked through in analysis, as unrecognized transference becomes resistance
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXVII

quote: Analytic therapy works by making the unconscious conscious, filling gaps in memory, overcoming resistances, and thus liberating repressed libido from its pathological fixations to become available for reality satisfactions
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXVII-XXVIII

quote: Psycho-analytic therapy does not create something new but removes obstacles (repressions, resistances) that prevent normal development, allowing libido to flow into more adaptive channels
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXVIII

quote: The analyst must maintain neutrality and abstinence, neither gratifying the patient's wishes nor imposing his own values, serving as a mirror that reflects only what is shown to him
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXVII-XXVIII

quote: Hysterical symptoms result from the conversion of psychical excitation into somatic innervation, with the symptom expressing a repressed sexual phantasy through a bodily manifestation
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXIV

quote: Obsessional neurosis differs from hysteria in employing different defense mechanisms: instead of conversion, it uses displacement, reaction-formation, isolation, and undoing to manage repressed impulses
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XVI-XVII

quote: In obsessional neurosis, regression typically goes back to the sadistic-anal stage, producing characteristic ambivalence, omnipotence of thoughts, and ritualistic behaviors
The choice of neurosis (whether someone develops hysteria, obsessional neurosis, or another form) depends primarily on the point of fixation to which libido regresses when frustrated in adult life

Perversions represent direct expressions of component instincts that have not been subordinated to genital organization, whereas neuroses are 'the negative of perversions'—the same instincts repressed rather than directly gratified
Narcissism represents a stage in libidinal development where the subject takes his own body and ego as love-object before directing love toward external objects
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXVI

quote: In narcissistic neuroses (including dementia praecox, paranoia, and melancholia), libido is withdrawn from external objects and turned back upon the ego, making transference impossible and these conditions largely inaccessible to psycho-analytic treatment
source: INTRODUCTORY LECTURES ON PSYCHO-ANALYSIS, LECTURE XXVI

quote: Sublimation is the process by which sexual instinctual energy is deflected from sexual aims and directed toward socially valuable achievements in art, science, and culture, providing an alternative to repression
The capacity for sublimation varies considerably between individuals, and insufficient capacity for sublimation is an important factor in neurosis predisposition

Identification is the earliest form of emotional tie with another person and plays a crucial role in the formation of the ego, as the child assimilates aspects of important figures (particularly parents) into his own personality

The superego (conscience, ego-ideal) forms through identification with parental authority, internalizing their prohibitions and commands, and thereafter exercises internal moral censorship independent of external authority

Guilt (unconscious sense of guilt) arises from conflict between ego and superego, particularly when forbidden oedipal wishes persist in the unconscious, and this guilt can manifest as need for punishment leading to self-sabotage
Repetition compulsion drives individuals to repeat painful experiences, traumas, and unsatisfying patterns of behavior, suggesting forces beyond the pleasure principle

Resistance arises from the ego's need to maintain repressions and avoid the unpleasure that would result from becoming conscious of repressed material; this resistance must be systematically overcome in analysis

The fundamental rule of psycho-analysis requires the patient to say everything that comes to mind without selection or criticism (free association), no matter how trivial, embarrassing, or apparently irrelevant

Interpretation should be given only when the material is near consciousness and strong resistance has been overcome; premature interpretation provokes increased resistance and is therapeutically counterproductive

Working-through requires repeated examination of resistances and conflicts from different angles over time; intellectual understanding alone (intellectual insight) is insufficient for therapeutic change
Psycho-analysis cannot abbreviate treatment or produce rapid cures because the pathological structures took years to form and require correspondingly lengthy work to dissolve

The goal of psycho-analytic treatment is to strengthen the ego, expand its organization, make it independent of the superego, widen its field of perception, and extend its organization so that it can appropriate fresh portions of the id
Psycho-analysis is not a Weltanschauung (world-view) but a specialized scientific method for investigating unconscious mental processes; it accepts the worldview of science in general

Bisexuality (constitutional presence of both masculine and feminine dispositions) is universal in humans and plays a role in all neurotic conflict, as repression often targets the opposite-sex component of the personality

The castration complex in boys (fear of losing the penis as punishment) and penis envy in girls (resentment at lacking the male organ) are universal phenomena arising from phallic stage experiences and profoundly shape subsequent development

Female sexual development is more complicated than male development because girls must change both their leading erotogenic zone (from clitoris to vagina) and their love-object (from mother to father), making them more vulnerable to developmental disturbances

The concept of psychical reality distinguishes the reality of unconscious phantasies and wishes, which have causal efficacy in producing symptoms, from material reality; phantasies are psychically real even when materially false

The economic point of view in metapsychology concerns quantities of psychical energy (cathexis), their distribution, and transformations; symptoms represent particular distributions of libidinal energy
The dynamic point of view concerns the forces and conflicts between psychical agencies (ego, id, superego) and between instinctual impulses and defenses
The topographical point of view concerns the organization of mental processes into systems (conscious, preconscious, unconscious) with different characteristics and laws of functioning
Memory-traces are organized in multiple registrations and systems; what becomes conscious is only the most recent version, while earlier versions persist in the unconscious and may remain active
Preconscious processes can become conscious through attention, while unconscious processes are barred from consciousness by repression and can only become conscious after the lifting of repression

The unconscious system operates according to primary process (disregarding logic, allowing contradictions, timeless, replacing external by psychical reality), while the preconscious/conscious operates by secondary process (logical, temporal, reality-oriented)

Affects and emotions become detached from their original ideational content through repression and may attach to different ideas, explaining why people often have strong feelings whose origin they cannot identify
Screen memories are apparently innocent childhood memories that conceal more significant repressed material through symbolic substitution or condensation of multiple memories
Childhood amnesia (the inability to remember experiences before age 3-5) results from repression of infantile sexual experiences and represents the foundation for later neurotic amnesias

Psychical conflict is inevitable in humans because sexual instincts demand satisfaction while ego instincts require adaptation to reality and social norms, making some form of instinct modification (repression, sublimation, or compromise) necessary

The sense of reality undergoes development from initial hallucinatory wish-fulfillment (where wished-for objects are simply hallucinated) to reality-testing (where the ego learns to distinguish perception from phantasy)
Attention is an ego function that scans both external perceptions and internal processes, directing cathexis toward significant stimuli and away from irrelevant ones
Judgment is a later ego function that decides whether a given idea is true or false (correspondence with reality) and whether a given object should be taken in or spat out (acceptance or rejection)
Thought originated as a substitute for hallucinatory wish-fulfillment, allowing experimental action with small amounts of psychical energy before committing to real action
Inhibition is a restriction of ego function imposed to avoid conflict with id, superego, or external reality; different inhibitions arise depending on which agency is being appeased
Sexual symbolism is remarkably limited and constant across individuals and cultures, suggesting phylogenetic inheritance rather than individual creation
Neurotic symptoms regularly have multiple determinants (overdetermination), expressing several different unconscious meanings simultaneously through a single manifestation
The somatic compliance in hysteria refers to the body's provision of a suitable location for symptom expression based on previous illness, constitutional factors, or symbolic appropriateness
Hysterical identification allows a person to express through symptoms not only her own repressed wishes but also those of other people with whom she identifies (as in epidemic hysteria)
The economic factor (quantity of libidinal energy) is as important as qualitative factors in determining symptom formation; sometimes quantitative increase alone can produce pathology

Primal phantasies (primal scene, castration, seduction) show such regularity and universality that they must be regarded as phylogenetic schemata, with individual experience merely filling in inherited structures

The compulsion neurotic's omnipotence of thoughts treats mental acts as equivalent to physical acts, believing that thinking something can make it happen or prevent it, showing regression to animistic thinking

Reaction-formation produces traits that are the opposite of repressed instinctual aims (e.g., excessive cleanliness as defense against anal-erotic impulses, excessive sympathy as defense against cruelty)

Isolation in obsessional neurosis separates an idea from its affect, allowing the idea to remain conscious while the associated feeling is repressed, producing apparently emotionless thoughts about disturbing content
Undoing is an obsessional mechanism where a second action symbolically cancels or negates a first action, as if the first had not occurred (e.g., compulsive rituals)

Ambivalence (simultaneous love and hate toward the same object) is particularly intense at the sadistic-anal stage and is characteristic of obsessional neurosis where it produces doubting, indecision, and opposing compulsions

The talking cure works because putting things into words creates an avenue from unconscious to conscious, and words themselves are derivatives of preconscious processes that can bridge the gap between systems

Acting out is the tendency to discharge unconscious impulses in action rather than remembering them, particularly manifest in transference where patients repeat rather than recollect childhood patterns
The negative therapeutic reaction occurs when clinical improvement produces increased guilt and self-punishment in some patients, paradoxically making them worse when treatment succeeds

Psycho-analysis can be applied beyond therapy to understanding cultural products (art, literature, myth, religion), social phenomena, and normal psychology, making it a general psychology of mental functioning

The artist creates as substitute satisfaction for repressed wishes, using his works to express unconscious phantasies in socially acceptable form, achieving both personal gratification and social recognition

Religion represents a collective obsessional neurosis arising from the same conflicts (oedipal guilt, ambivalence toward father) that produce individual obsessional neurosis, offering ritualized defenses against forbidden wishes
Education should aim to help the child find a middle path between unrestricted instinctual expression (which would make civilization impossible) and excessive repression (which produces neurosis)

The therapeutic aim is not to eliminate conflict but to help the ego make better choices about managing conflicts, replacing pathological solutions (symptoms) with adaptive ones (reality satisfactions, sublimations)
Character traits are precipitates of abandoned object-cathexes, formed when the ego responds to loss of an object by identifying with it and taking on its characteristics

The constitutional factor (innate variations in instinctual strength, capacity for sublimation, ego resilience) always plays a role in neurosis aetiology and explains why similar experiences produce different outcomes in different individuals

Dementia praecox (schizophrenia) involves profound narcissistic withdrawal where libido is retracted from objects and turned inward to such degree that reality-contact is lost and internal phantasy world becomes predominant

Melancholia results when loss of an object is handled not by mourning but by narcissistic identification, with the ego setting up the lost object inside itself and then directing ambivalent feelings (particularly hatred) against this internalized object, thus attacking itself

Normal mourning and pathological melancholia share the feature of reaction to object-loss, but mourning works through the loss gradually while melancholia involves regression to narcissistic identification
Paranoia results from projection of repressed homosexual impulses onto a persecutor, transforming 'I love him' (forbidden) into 'He hates me' (acceptable but persecutory)
The theory of instincts is our mythology; instincts are mythical entities, magnificent in their indefiniteness, yet indispensable for understanding mental processes

Psycho-analysis has no Weltanschauung of its own but shares the scientific Weltanschauung, characterized by emphasis on external world, rejection of illusions, and acceptance that our picture of reality is incomplete and revisable
The pleasure-unpleasure principle governs primary process, seeking immediate discharge of tension (pleasure) and avoiding increase in tension (unpleasure), without regard for reality or consequences
Reality adaptation requires that pleasure be postponed, immediate satisfactions renounced, and unpleasure temporarily tolerated in service of greater long-term advantage
The sexual instinct is particularly resistant to education and taming by the reality principle, maintaining its pressure toward immediate satisfaction even when ego interests require renunciation

The common feature of all psychical phenomena studied by psycho-analysis (dreams, parapraxes, symptoms, jokes) is that they represent compromises between opposing mental forces, making compromise-formation the fundamental pattern of unconscious expression

Sexuality in the psycho-analytic sense (sexuelle, Eros) is broader than genital sexuality, encompassing all pleasurable bodily sensations and the full range of libidinal strivings including infantile, component, and perverse sexualities

The distinction between ego-instincts (self-preservative) and sexual instincts (species-preservative) represents the fundamental duality driving psychical conflict, though this distinction may require future revision
The incompatibility between civilization's demands and instinctual nature creates universal discontent, making some degree of neurosis or sublimation inevitable in civilized life

Dreams are the normal prototype of all pathological mental products—they show in miniature all the mechanisms (regression, displacement, condensation, symbolism, compromise-formation) that operate in neurotic symptom-formation

The forgetting of proper names demonstrates that apparently arbitrary failures of memory follow deterministic laws, being caused by specific interfering thoughts even when the person is unaware of these thoughts
Slips of the tongue often reveal what the speaker really thinks or feels despite conscious intentions to conceal it, making them a form of inadvertent self-betrayal

Adler's individual psychology represents a defection from psycho-analysis because it denies infantile sexuality, eliminates the unconscious, rejects repression, and reduces all motivation to ego-striving (will to power), thereby abandoning the foundations that define psychoanalysis
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: Jung's analytic psychology dissolves psycho-analysis by reinterpreting libido as general life-energy rather than sexual energy, treating the Oedipus complex as symbolic rather than literal, and denying the reality of infantile sexuality
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: The controversies with Adler and Jung demonstrate that the core principles of psycho-analysis (unconscious, repression, infantile sexuality, transference) are non-negotiable—anyone rejecting these cannot properly call their work psychoanalysis
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: Psycho-analysis began in 1895-1896 with Freud's recognition that hysterical symptoms have sexual meaning and arise from repressed sexual traumas, though the seduction theory was later revised
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: The recognition that patients' reports of childhood seduction were often phantasies rather than real events led to the discovery of infantile sexuality and psychical reality, representing a crucial theoretical advance
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: Free association emerged as the fundamental method when Freud abandoned hypnosis, asking patients to report everything that came to mind, thereby providing access to unconscious material through the chain of associations
source: ON THE HISTORY OF THE PSYCHO-ANALYTIC MOVEMENT

quote: The interpretation of a dream requires that the dreamer associate freely to each element, not that the analyst impose symbolic meanings; fixed symbolic translations without associations are insufficient
Children's dreams are typically undistorted wish-fulfillments because the censorship is not yet strong, showing directly what later becomes disguised in adult dreams

Dreams incorporate recent waking impressions (day-residues) not because these are important in themselves but because they provide convenient material through which deeper unconscious wishes can express themselves
The manifest dream is typically much shorter than the latent dream-thoughts it represents, demonstrating massive condensation through which multiple meanings are compressed into single images
Dreams show preference for representing abstract relationships and logical connections through spatial arrangements, simultaneity, or transformations rather than through language
The interpretation of symptoms follows the same principles as dream interpretation, treating the symptom as a manifest content concealing latent meanings that emerge through patient's associations
Symptom-formation serves as a substitute satisfaction when normal satisfaction is blocked, but the satisfaction gained is distorted, partial, and inseparable from suffering
The strength required to maintain repression is drawn from the ego's supply of energy, weakening the ego and reducing its capacity for adaptation and sublimation
Successful analysis liberates the energy bound in maintaining repressions, making it available for ego functions, adaptation, and sublimation
Not all conflicts produce neurosis—only when the ego's capacity is overwhelmed, when regression to infantile fixation-points occurs, and when substitute satisfactions fail to provide adequate relief

The therapeutic process requires not just intellectual insight but emotional working-through, where resistances are repeatedly encountered and overcome until the patient can genuinely accept and integrate the unconscious material

Anxiety (Angst) constitutes the fundamental problem of the neuroses, the nodal point at which the most various and important questions converge, and a riddle whose solution would cast a flood of light upon our whole mental existence
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXV: Anxiety (1917)

quote: Three types of anxiety must be distinguished: realistic anxiety (normal fear of external dangers), neurotic anxiety (fear disproportionate to actual danger), and moral anxiety (conscience or superego anxiety)
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXV: Anxiety (1917)

quote: Realistic anxiety appears entirely rational and intelligible, representing a reaction to the perception of an external danger - an injury that is expected and foreseen; it is connected with the flight reflex and may be regarded as an expression of the self-preservative instinct
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXV: Anxiety (1917)

quote: Anxiety prepares the organism for danger by heightening sensory attention and motor tension; the anxiety-affect consists of motor discharge (trembling, perspiration, palpitations) and sensations of unpleasure
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXV: Anxiety (1917)

quote: Neurotic anxiety manifests in three forms: free-floating general apprehensiveness (expectant anxiety), anxiety attached to specific phobic objects or situations, and anxiety in hysteria and other neuroses appearing without apparent connection to danger
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXV: Anxiety (1917)

quote: In anxiety neurosis, libido is turned into anxiety when denied normal discharge: the anxiety represents transformed libido which has not been employed psychically but discharged in the form of anxiety
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXV: Anxiety (1917)

quote: Phobic anxiety differs from expectant anxiety by being attached to specific objects or situations, but shares with it the characteristic of anxiety disproportionate to actual danger; the phobic object serves as a condition under which anxiety is permitted release
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXV: Anxiety (1917)

quote: The ego creates the phobia as an attempt to escape from the libidinal demand made by the id; the phobic avoidance protects the ego from experiencing anxiety by avoiding the situation that would provoke the forbidden libidinal impulse
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXV: Anxiety (1917)

quote: Anxiety in hysteria and obsessional neurosis accompanies symptoms: the anxiety is the quota of affect that has become liberated through the process of repression and which would otherwise have been employed in some other way if it had not been repressed
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXV: Anxiety (1917)

quote: The transformation of libido into anxiety occurs through two mechanisms: direct transformation when libido cannot be discharged (anxiety neurosis), and liberation of affect quota when repression occurs (hysteria and obsessional neurosis)
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXV: Anxiety (1917)

quote: The equation 'repression = anxiety' is fundamentally correct: repression arises from the ego's anxiety-readiness, and the repressed libidinal impulse is transformed into the anxiety which accompanies neurotic symptoms
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXV: Anxiety (1917)

quote: The right to separate ego-instincts from sexual instincts cannot be shaken: it is implied in the existence of sexual life as a distinct activity of the individual, though the depth and importance of this separation must be determined by observing how differently the two behave
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXVI: Libido Theory and Narcissism (1917)

quote: If hunger and thirst (self-preservative instincts) are unsatisfied, the result is never their transformation into anxiety, whereas the changing of unsatisfied libido into anxiety is among the best known and most frequently observed of phenomena - demonstrating a fundamental difference between ego and sexual instincts
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXVI: Libido Theory and Narcissism (1917)

quote: Libido is the energy of the sexual instincts directed to an object; it is qualitatively distinct from the energy of the ego-instincts and is capable of transformations, displacements, and regressions not observed in self-preservative impulses
source: Introductory Lectures on Psycho-Analysis, Lecture XXVI: Libido Theory and Narcissism (1917)

quote: Narcissism is the libidinal complement to egoism of the self-preservative instinct: it is the libidinal cathexis of the ego, representing a state in which the ego itself is taken as the love-object
